target.title: fix(agent): abort accepted gateway runs on signal
target.body:
## Problem

`openclaw agent` could keep waiting after an external `SIGTERM` or `SIGINT` while a Gateway-backed agent run had already been accepted server-side. For cron, systemd, and other supervisors, that meant the CLI process could be interrupted without asking the Gateway to cancel the in-flight run it had just started.

## Change and value

This PR propagates caller abort signals through the Gateway client path, records the accepted run id while the CLI waits for the final response, and has `openclaw agent` send `chat.abort` before exiting on `SIGTERM`/`SIGINT`. The abort request reuses the same Gateway identity as the original agent run, including the backend/admin authority used for `--model` override runs, so cleanup remains authorized for both default and model override execution.

## Who is affected, and who is not

- Affected: operators running `openclaw agent` through cron, systemd, shell `timeout`, CI jobs, or any wrapper that may send `SIGTERM`/`SIGINT` while a Gateway-backed run is active.
- Affected: Gateway client callers that need abort propagation and accepted-run visibility while waiting for a final response.
- Not changed: Gateway cron's existing isolated-run watchdogs, process-supervisor hard-kill behavior, provider cancellation semantics after a process has already been killed, or broader launcher/orphan cleanup.

## Why now

Issue #71710 is still current on `main`: server-side accepted-run abort support exists, but the one-shot agent CLI did not bridge process signals into the Gateway request or follow up with `chat.abort` for the accepted run. This PR closes that caller-side lifecycle gap without adding a public knob.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #71710
- Related #71214
- Related #77481
- Related #82403
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Implementation

- `GatewayClient.request` now accepts an optional caller `AbortSignal` and an `onAccepted` callback for callers that wait for accepted Gateway runs.
- `callGateway` forwards the caller signal and accepted-run callback into `GatewayClient`.
- `agent-via-gateway` installs `SIGTERM`/`SIGINT` listeners around Gateway-backed and local embedded CLI runs, aborts the waiting request, and sends `chat.abort` for the accepted run id and resolved session key.
- The follow-up `chat.abort` uses the same Gateway identity as the original agent request. Default runs keep CLI identity; `--model` override runs keep backend/admin authority.
- Abort errors short-circuit before embedded Gateway fallback classification, so a user-requested abort cannot be retried as a fresh embedded run.
- CLI docs keep the external-supervisor `timeout -k` hard-kill backstop guidance and call out the same SIGTERM-plus-grace-window shape for systemd units.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Gateway-backed `openclaw agent` now treats `SIGTERM`/`SIGINT` as a caller abort, rejects the waiting request, and sends `chat.abort` for the accepted Gateway run id before the CLI exits. The same behavior is covered for default CLI identity and for `--model` override runs that require backend/admin abort authority.
- Real environment tested: remote Crabbox/GCP Spot VM, `e2-standard-8`, `us-east1-b`, lease `cbx_5e7f0ad24205` / slug `tidal-hermit`, Node `v24.15.0`, pnpm `11.1.0`, PR head `533728f0a26596b5d315c527621075735456dbbf`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: on the remote VM, installed dependencies, ran `pnpm build`, started the built `dist/index.js` Gateway plus a temporary OpenAI-compatible provider that holds `/v1/responses` open, then ran the actual CLI twice: default Gateway CLI identity and `--model mock-openai/gpt-5.5`. After each provider request was in flight, the harness sent `SIGTERM` and verified the provider stream closed and the CLI exited with the Gateway abort error. The same VM then ran the focused Vitest files and `pnpm check:changed`.
- Evidence after fix:

```text
proof.ok=true
proof.repo=/work/crabbox/cbx_5e7f0ad24205/openclaw-71710-sigterm
gateway.url=ws://127.0.0.1:42817
provider.url=http://127.0.0.1:35139/v1
provider.requests=2
default-cli.providerPath=/v1/responses
default-cli.providerClosed=true
default-cli.child.exit.code=1
default-cli.child.exit.signal=
default-cli.child.stderr.firstLine=AbortError: gateway request aborted for agent
model-override.providerPath=/v1/responses
model-override.providerClosed=true
model-override.child.exit.code=1
model-override.child.exit.signal=
model-override.child.stderr.firstLine=AbortError: gateway request aborted for agent
```

- Observed result after fix: both actual CLI runs received `SIGTERM`, aborted the waiting Gateway request, closed the in-flight provider response, and exited with `AbortError: gateway request aborted for agent`. The focused tests also verify the accepted run id/session key are used for `chat.abort`, that the model override abort path preserves backend/admin authority, that `--local` runs receive the same abort signal, and that abort errors do not enter embedded fallback classification.
- What was not tested: a live production provider credential, real cron/systemd timing, or an OS-level forced `SIGKILL`. The proof used a temporary OpenAI-compatible provider on the remote VM to avoid external credentials while exercising the built Gateway, actual CLI process, Gateway request identity, accepted-run callback, and abort path. The docs continue to recommend `timeout -k` or an equivalent supervisor backstop for hard-kill cases.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts`, `src/gateway/call.test.ts`, `src/gateway/client.watchdog.test.ts`.
- Scenario the tests lock in: accepted Gateway agent run plus `SIGTERM` interrupts the CLI, aborts the waiting request, sends `chat.abort` for the accepted run id, preserves the original Gateway identity for abort authorization, forwards the abort signal to local embedded runs, and removes signal listeners.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises the three ownership boundaries changed by this PR without requiring a real production provider or long-running scheduler job.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`openclaw agent` now handles `SIGTERM`/`SIGINT` for Gateway-backed runs by interrupting the waiting request and asking the Gateway to abort an already accepted run id before exiting. External cron/systemd wrappers should still keep a hard-kill backstop such as `timeout -k 60 600 openclaw agent ...` or an equivalent SIGTERM grace window.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
external SIGTERM -> CLI listener absent -> Gateway request/run can remain in flight

After:
external SIGTERM -> CLI AbortSignal -> Gateway request rejects -> CLI chat.abort(runId) -> exit
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: on signal interruption only, the CLI may send an existing authenticated Gateway `chat.abort` request for the accepted run id and resolved session key. This reuses existing Gateway auth and abort semantics. Default runs use CLI identity; `--model` override runs use the same backend/admin authority as the original model override request so the abort is authorized without adding a new scope.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: remote Crabbox/GCP Linux VM for build, real Gateway/CLI/provider proof, focused tests, and `pnpm check:changed`.
- Runtime/container: Node `v24.15.0`, pnpm `11.1.0` remotely.
- Model/provider: temporary OpenAI-compatible provider on the VM for real CLI/Gateway proof.
- Integration/channel: Gateway-backed `openclaw agent` CLI path.
- Relevant config: `OPENCLAW_HANDSHAKE_TIMEOUT_MS` unset for deterministic timeout expectations; Vitest workers limited to 1 for focused tests.

### Commands and results

```text
pnpm build
# passed

timeout 300s node .crabbox-proof/openclaw-84381-real-gateway-proof.mjs
# passed; proof.ok=true

env -u OPENCLAW_HANDSHAKE_TIMEOUT_MS OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=90000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.commands.config.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts
# 19 passed

env -u OPENCLAW_HANDSHAKE_TIMEOUT_MS OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=60000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-core.config.ts src/gateway/call.test.ts
# 84 passed

env -u OPENCLAW_HANDSHAKE_TIMEOUT_MS OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=60000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-client.config.ts src/gateway/client.watchdog.test.ts
# 11 passed

env -u OPENCLAW_HANDSHAKE_TIMEOUT_MS OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm check:changed
# exit 0; command=7m6.041s; total=7m37.28s
```

`pnpm check:changed` deliberately fail-safed to all lanes because the remote sync included the temporary untracked proof harness, so the remote gate was broader than the tracked PR diff.

### Expected

- CLI signal bridge aborts the waiting Gateway request and sends `chat.abort` for the accepted run id.
- Abort cleanup uses the same Gateway identity as the original accepted run.
- Local embedded runs receive the same abort signal instead of swallowing process signals.
- `callGateway` forwards `AbortSignal` and accepted callbacks into `GatewayClient.request`.
- Changed-file checks pass.

### Actual

- Real Gateway/CLI/provider proof passed for both default CLI identity and `--model` override identity.
- Focused tests passed.
- Remote `pnpm build` and `pnpm check:changed` passed.
- Crabbox release cleanup was checked afterward; no matching `crabbox-*` instances remained listed.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified the issue is still current on `main`: server-side accepted-run abort machinery exists, but the one-shot `agent` CLI did not propagate process signals into the Gateway request or abort the accepted run.
- Verified the first implementation's cleanup authorization gap and fixed it by reusing the original Gateway request identity for `chat.abort`.
- Verified Claude's local/embedded abort concern against source and added a focused `--local` signal propagation test.
- Verified focused tests and real behavior proof on the exact pushed PR head `533728f0a26596b5d315c527621075735456dbbf`.

target.title: Audit config backups for retained plaintext secrets
target.body:
## Summary
- scan adjacent config snapshots such as openclaw.json.bak, numbered backups, pre-update snapshots, rejected snapshots, and clobbered snapshots during secrets audit
- report plaintext secret targets found in those snapshots without echoing secret values
- add regression coverage for plaintext API keys retained in config backups

Refs #11829.

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed**: `openclaw secrets audit` now detects plaintext secrets retained in adjacent `openclaw.json.*` config snapshots, while still resolving the active config SecretRef normally and without echoing the plaintext value.

**Real environment tested**: Local macOS source checkout at `2a3b267e41f71b034627100bcdf47ff54030d267`, Node `v25.2.1`, pnpm `11.1.0`, using the real `pnpm openclaw secrets audit --json` CLI path after the launcher built the stale `dist` output.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Created a temporary OpenClaw state dir with an active `openclaw.json` using `{ source: "env", provider: "default", id: "OPENAI_API_KEY" }`, plus an adjacent `openclaw.json.bak` retaining a sample plaintext provider key. Then ran:
```bash
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=.artifacts/secrets-proof-AM4OWZ/state \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=.artifacts/secrets-proof-AM4OWZ/state/openclaw.json \
OPENAI_API_KEY=env-proof-value \
pnpm openclaw secrets audit --json
```

**Evidence after fix**: Copied live CLI output excerpt from the command above:
```json
{
  "status": "findings",
  "resolution": {
    "refsChecked": 1,
    "skippedExecRefs": 0,
    "resolvabilityComplete": true
  },
  "filesScanned": [
    ".artifacts/secrets-proof-AM4OWZ/state/openclaw.json",
    ".artifacts/secrets-proof-AM4OWZ/state/openclaw.json.bak"
  ],
  "summary": {
    "plaintextCount": 1,
    "unresolvedRefCount": 0,
    "shadowedRefCount": 0,
    "legacyResidueCount": 0
  },
  "findings": [
    {
      "code": "PLAINTEXT_FOUND",
      "severity": "warn",
      "file": ".artifacts/secrets-proof-AM4OWZ/state/openclaw.json.bak",
      "jsonPath": "models.providers.openai.apiKey",
      "message": "Config backup contains plaintext at models.providers.openai.apiKey. Remove stale snapshots or rotate the credential if this backup may have left the host.",
      "provider": "openai"
    }
  ]
}
```

**Observed result after fix**: The audit scanned both `openclaw.json` and `openclaw.json.bak`, resolved the active env SecretRef (`refsChecked: 1`, `unresolvedRefCount: 0`), and reported exactly one plaintext finding against the backup path/jsonPath. The plaintext key value did not appear in the JSON report.

**What was not tested**: None.

## Tests
- node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/secrets/audit.test.ts
- pnpm exec oxlint src/secrets/audit.ts src/secrets/audit.test.ts src/secrets/storage-scan.ts
- pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/secrets/audit.ts src/secrets/audit.test.ts src/secrets/storage-scan.ts
- pnpm tsgo:core
- pnpm tsgo:core:test

- npm exec --yes pnpm@11.1.0 -- exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/models-config.applies-config-env-vars.test.ts src/agents/models-config.merge.test.ts src/agents/models-config.providers.auth-provenance.test.ts src/agents/model-auth-markers.test.ts

## Security Impact
- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? Yes
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

This reduces token persistence by stripping plaintext apiKey values before writing models.json, while preserving marker-based runtime auth references.

## Notes
I did not verify a full interactive OpenClaw agent runtime session with live provider credentials. The regression tests exercise the affected planner serialization boundary directly.

target.title: fix(browser): drop unused navigation requireRef import
target.body:
## Summary
- Remove the phantom `requireRef` import from browser navigation command registration.
- Delete the `void requireRef` side-effect expression that existed only to silence unused-import lint.
- Leave `requireRef` exported from `shared.ts` for the modules that actually need it.

Fixes #83878

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `extensions/browser/src/cli/browser-cli-actions-input/register.navigation.ts` imported `requireRef` only to reference it through `void requireRef`; this kept a dead import alive without any real browser navigation behavior depending on it.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, Node.js v22.22.0, branch `fix/browser-navigation-drop-requireref-83878`.

Exact steps or command run after fix: Verified the edited source file no longer references `requireRef`, while `shared.ts` still exports `requireRef` for real callers:

```console
$ python3 - <<'PY'
from pathlib import Path
path = Path('extensions/browser/src/cli/browser-cli-actions-input/register.navigation.ts')
text = path.read_text()
print('requireRef occurrences in register.navigation.ts:', text.count('requireRef'))
for i, line in enumerate(text.splitlines(), 1):
    if 'shared.js' in line or 'requireRef' in line:
        print(f'{i}: {line}')
PY
$ python3 - <<'PY'
from pathlib import Path
path = Path('extensions/browser/src/cli/browser-cli-actions-input/shared.ts')
for i, line in enumerate(path.read_text().splitlines(), 1):
    if 'function requireRef' in line:
        print(f'{i}: {line}')
PY
```

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the command above:

```console
requireRef occurrences in register.navigation.ts: 0
4: import { callBrowserRequest, type BrowserParentOpts } from "../browser-cli-shared.js";
6: import { resolveBrowserActionContext } from "./shared.js";
66: export function requireRef(ref: string | undefined) {
```

Observed result after fix: The browser navigation registration file now imports only the shared helper it actually uses, and the `void requireRef` dead-import workaround is gone. The shared helper remains available from `shared.ts`, so this change only removes the unrelated phantom dependency from `register.navigation.ts`.

What was not tested: I did not run a live browser navigation command because the change is import-graph cleanup only. I also did not complete `pnpm lint:extensions:no-relative-outside-package` because pnpm attempted a dependency install and timed out on registry downloads in this environment.

## Test plan
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/browser/src/cli/browser-cli-actions-input/register.navigation.ts`
- `git diff --check`

Debug environment variables like OPENCLAW_DEBUG_INGRESS_TIMING could be 
accidentally enabled in production, potentially exposing sensitive timing 
information or increasing attack surface.

## Solution

- Create centralized debug-env.ts helper for consistent debug access patterns
- Document all debug environment variables for security auditing

## Files Changed
- src/agents/model-catalog.ts: Update shouldLogModelCatalogTiming()
- src/agents/debug-env.ts (new): Centralized debug utilities

## Testing
- [x] Debug flags disabled in production
- [x] Debug flags work in development

This PR adds a C/GTK4/libadwaita Linux companion app under `apps/linux`, a GTK3 tray helper, Linux gateway/systemd/RPC/onboarding management surfaces and tests, a `LINUX_APP` client id, and an `agents.update` default-agent contract change.

target.title: feat: cute GTK-native Linux App (#75)
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: - OpenClaw did not have an in-repo native Linux companion app, so Linux users were missing a first-class desktop surface for gateway lifecycle, diagnostics, tray/status behavior, onboarding, and key operational management flows. - The existing Linux direction in #75 called for a GTK-native companion rather than treating Linux as only a wrapper or dashboard handoff. - Why it matters: - Linux is a real target platform in the issue, and the lack of a native companion left users without parity on core desktop workflows such as service state visibility, tray/status integration, local diagnostics, and direct management of gateway-backed sections. - Without a native app, Linux-specific concerns such as systemd user-service integration, Ubuntu GNOME tray behavior, local-vs-remote resource access, and distro-native dependency flow were not handled in a coherent product surface. - What changed: - Added a new native Linux companion app under `apps/linux` using C, GTK4, and Libadwaita, plus a private GTK3 Ayatana/AppIndicator tray helper for Ubuntu GNOME tray/status integration. - Added a native gateway client stack for Linux: config loading, HTTP health probing, authenticated WebSocket connection, typed RPC mutation helpers, gateway data adapters, and controller-based UI sections. - Added native operational management surfaces for Channels, Skills, Sessions, Cron, and Instances, including config-safe editing, locality-aware transcript/log opening, pairing actions, and richer management flows previously absent on Linux. - Added Linux runtime/readiness modeling, onboarding, diagnostics, notifications, systemd integration, and focused automated tests covering parser behavior, mutation request shapes, RPC lifecycle handling, config rebuild safety, runtime/readiness logic, and helper behavior. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): - This PR does not add distro packaging artifacts such as `.deb`, `.rpm`, or Snap packages. - This PR does not attempt to solve the Windows side. - This PR does not claim full macOS parity across every desktop capability; it establishes the native Linux companion foundation and a substantial in-app management surface. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [x] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [x] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [x] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #75 - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`. - Root cause: - N/A - Missing detection / guardrail: - N/A - Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): - N/A - Why this regressed now: - N/A - If unknown, what was ruled out: - N/A ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should have caught this. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [ ] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - N/A - Scenario the test should lock in: - N/A - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: - N/A - Existing test that already covers this (if any): - N/A - If no new test is added, why not: - N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). If none, write `None`. - Linux now has a native GTK4 + Libadwaita companion app in-repo. - Ubuntu GNOME users get tray/status integration through the companion plus the private AppIndicator helper. - The Linux app can discover and work with the user `openclaw-gateway.service` lifecycle through native systemd integration. - The app surfaces first-run progression clearly across the main readiness states: - before `openclaw setup` - after `openclaw setup` but before gateway install - after gateway install with the service active and the gateway answering - The app surfaces runtime/readiness/onboarding/diagnostics states directly in the Linux UI. - Channels can now be inspected and managed in-app, including probe, logout, config editing, and WhatsApp QR linking flow. - Skills can now be filtered and managed in-app, including enable/disable, install, and env/API-key updates. - Sessions can now be managed in-app, including patch/reset/compact/delete and log access. - Cron can now be managed in-app, including scheduler status, recent runs, create/edit, run-now, enable/disable, delete, and transcript access. - Instances now expose richer remote node details, pending pairing approval/reject flows, and copy-debug-info behavior. - Log/transcript opening is locality-aware: local resources open locally when available, otherwise the UI falls back safely. - Channel config editing now validates edited JSON, preserves unrelated config keys, and saves using `baseHash`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: [user on Linux] -> [CLI / dashboard / partial prototype paths] -> [limited lifecycle visibility and little or no native management] After: [user on Linux] -> [native GTK4 companion app] -> [systemd + HTTP + authenticated WebSocket runtime model] -> [tray / diagnostics / onboarding / management sections] -> [native actions for channels, skills, sessions, cron, instances] ```` ## Security Impact (required) * New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: * The Linux companion introduces a new native desktop surface that can read local runtime context, inspect service state, connect to the local gateway over HTTP/WebSocket, edit config, open local logs/transcripts when available, and trigger gateway-backed management mutations. * Risk is mitigated by keeping the runtime model explicit, separating systemd discovery from gateway RPC transport, validating config JSON before save, preserving unrelated config content during rebuild, using `baseHash` on config writes, hardening request shapes through typed mutation helpers, and covering lifecycle/parsing/request-shape behavior in targeted tests. * Local-vs-remote resource access is handled explicitly so the app does not blindly assume every gateway-returned path is locally openable. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment * OS: * Ubuntu 24.04 and Ubuntu 26.04 GNOME * Runtime/container: * Bare-metal / local user-session workflow with systemd user service * Model/provider: * Local gateway-backed companion flow; model/provider depends on gateway config * Integration/channel (if any): * systemd user service, HTTP health, authenticated gateway WebSocket, GTK4 + Libadwaita, Ayatana/AppIndicator * Relevant config (redacted): * Fresh local OpenClaw config and user `openclaw-gateway.service`; secrets redacted ### Steps 1. Install Linux build dependencies and clone/build OpenClaw. 2. Build and run the Linux app with Meson. 3. Launch the Linux companion app before running `openclaw setup`. 4. Run `openclaw setup`, then relaunch or refresh the companion app. 5. Install and start the user gateway service. 6. Launch or refresh the Linux companion app again. 7. Exercise tray/status, diagnostics, onboarding/readiness, and the Dashboard / General / Channels / Skills / Sessions / Cron / Instances sections. 8. Run the Linux test suite. ### Expected * Before setup, the app reports the missing setup/runtime state clearly. * After setup but before gateway install, the app reports configured-but-not-installed service state clearly. * After gateway install/start, the app reports a healthy connected state, with diagnostics, dashboard, and general surfaces reflecting the running gateway. * The Linux app builds, launches, connects through the native runtime model, reflects systemd/gateway state accurately, and exposes native management flows for the major gateway-backed sections. * The Linux test suite passes. ### Actual * Matches expected in local verification. ## Evidence Attach at least one: * [x] Failing test/log before + passing after * [x] Trace/log snippets * [x] Screenshot/recording * [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What I personally verified (not just CI), and how: * Verified scenarios: * Built the Linux app from a clean checkout on Ubuntu 24.04 and Ubuntu 26.04 GNOME. * Ran the Meson test suite locally. * Verified tray helper spawn and tray/status behavior. * Verified systemd user-service discovery and lifecycle reflection. * Verified diagnostics/readiness/runtime presentation. * Verified first-run progression manually with screenshots: * app launched before `openclaw setup` * app after `openclaw setup` * app after gateway install/start with the gateway answering properly * Verified the healthy connected state manually against a fresh configured local gateway. * Verified additional Linux app surfaces manually, including Dashboard, General, and Diagnostics. * Verified the main Linux companion window and controller-backed sections load and refresh. * Verified native management flows across Channels, Skills, Sessions, Cron, and Instances at the implementation level. * Verified config editing flow behavior, including full-document rebuild and hash-aware save path. * Verified locality-aware log/transcript fallback behavior. * Edge cases checked: * Invalid config JSON aborts save. * Config rebuild preserves unrelated top-level keys and sibling channel config. * Mutation request shapes match the intended backend contract structure. * RPC lifecycle handling covers success/error/timeout/disconnect cleanup paths. * Tray-helper lifecycle and cleanup behavior were tightened. * Readiness states remain intelligible across the bootstrap sequence from no setup -> configured -> installed/running. * What I did **not** verify: * Official distro packaging artifacts (`.deb`, `.rpm`, Snap) are not included here. * Broad desktop-environment compatibility beyond the Ubuntu GNOME target path. * Full end-to-end parity for every macOS companion capability. ## Review Conversations * [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. * [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration * Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) * No * Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) * No * If yes, exact upgrade steps: * N/A ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. * Risk: * Linux desktop behavior can vary by distro and shell, especially around tray hosting. * Mitigation: * Target the initial implementation clearly at Ubuntu GNOME, use a private AppIndicator helper, and keep the helper boundary isolated from the GTK4 main process. * Risk: * The Linux companion now depends on a larger native runtime stack: systemd context, HTTP health, WebSocket RPC, and gateway payload parsing. * Mitigation: * Keep those concerns separated into dedicated modules and cover parser/RPC/mutation behavior with focused tests. * Risk: * Config editing could accidentally overwrite unrelated config content if the save path is too narrow. * Mitigation: * Load the full config document, edit only the relevant subtree, rebuild the full document, validate JSON before save, and send `baseHash` with `config.set`. * Risk: * Transcript/log opening can fail or behave incorrectly if the gateway resource is not local to the GTK host. * Mitigation: * Enforce locality-aware fallback logic and only open local resources directly when they are actually present and accessible. * Risk: * This is a large first-class app addition and therefore a large review surface. * Mitigation: * Keep the app organized into focused subsystems and section controllers, provide a documented Linux build/test path, and include targeted tests for the most contract-sensitive areas.

target.title: fix(cli): reject partially numeric --limit values in search commands
target.body:
## Summary
- Replace `Number.parseInt` with strict `Number()` + validation for `--limit` option in `plugins search` and `skills search` CLI commands.
- Rejects partially numeric values like `1abc`, decimals like `5.5`, and non-positive values like `0` or `-1` that were previously silently accepted.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: `openclaw plugins search` and `openclaw skills search` parse `--limit` with `Number.parseInt`, so partially numeric values like `1abc` are accepted as `1` instead of being rejected.

**Real environment tested**: Linux x86_64, Node.js v22.22.0. Pure JavaScript parsing logic, platform-independent.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch**:
```bash
cd openclaw-84473
node proof_repro.mjs
```
The script tests 6 inputs (`1abc`, `5.5`, `0`, `-1`, `10`, `1`) against the old parseInt parser and the new strict parser to verify rejection/acceptance behavior.

**Evidence after fix**:
```
=== CLI search --limit strict parsing fix (#84473) ===
Timestamp : 2026-05-20T10:06:04.223Z
Node.js   : v22.22.0
Platform  : linux x64

--- Test: various --limit values ---

Input: "1abc"
  BEFORE (parseInt): accepted as 1
  AFTER  (strict):   rejected: --limit must be a positive integer
  Expected: REJECT → PASS

Input: "5.5"
  BEFORE (parseInt): accepted as 5
  AFTER  (strict):   rejected: --limit must be a positive integer
  Expected: REJECT → PASS

Input: "0"
  BEFORE (parseInt): accepted as 0
  AFTER  (strict):   rejected: --limit must be a positive integer
  Expected: REJECT → PASS

Input: "-1"
  BEFORE (parseInt): accepted as -1
  AFTER  (strict):   rejected: --limit must be a positive integer
  Expected: REJECT → PASS

Input: "10"
  BEFORE (parseInt): accepted as 10
  AFTER  (strict):   accepted as 10
  Expected: ACCEPT → PASS

Input: "1"
  BEFORE (parseInt): accepted as 1
  AFTER  (strict):   accepted as 1
  Expected: ACCEPT → PASS

=== Result ===
All cases: PASS
```

**Observed result after fix**: Before fix: `Number.parseInt("1abc", 10)` returns `1`, silently accepting invalid input. After fix: strict `Number()` + validation rejects `"1abc"`, `"5.5"`, `"0"`, `"-1"` with error "--limit must be a positive integer". Valid integers `"10"` and `"1"` still accepted normally.

**What was not tested**: Live CLI invocation with `pnpm openclaw plugins search`. Other CLI commands that use `Number.parseInt` for different options (those are out of scope for this issue).

Fixes #84473

target.title: test(cron): document and test owner-only tool security boundary for isolated cron
target.body:
## Summary

Document and test the cron owner-only tool security boundary in `resolveCronOwnerOnlyToolAllowlist()`.

Isolated cron runs are executed with `senderIsOwner: false`. The `resolveCronOwnerOnlyToolAllowlist()` function determines which owner-only tools are granted to the cron agent. Only `cron` (for self-management like `deleteAfterRun`) is safe to auto-grant in unattended runs. Control-plane tools (`gateway`, `nodes`) must not be auto-granted.

### Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `run-executor.ts` | Extract `CRON_SAFE_OWNER_ONLY_TOOLS` constant, export function, use `isOwnerOnlyToolName()` + safe-list filter |
| `run-executor.owner-only-allowlist.test.ts` | 6 test cases covering the security boundary |

### What this does NOT fix

Issue #84141 reports that `exec` is missing in isolated cron despite `toolsAllow: ["exec"]`. The `exec` tool is **not** an owner-only tool (`isOwnerOnlyToolName("exec") === false`), so the owner-only allowlist is irrelevant to that bug. The exec regression is in a different code path (tool hydration for non-owner-only tools in isolated cron).

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.owner-only-allowlist.test.ts
 ✓ unit-fast (6 tests)
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  6 passed (6)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: The cron owner-only allowlist was undocumented, unexported, and untested. This PR makes the security boundary explicit with a named constant and focused tests.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, running the actual `resolveCronOwnerOnlyToolAllowlist` function from the source tree.

### Owner-only tool classification (tool-policy.ts)

```
cron:    isOwnerOnly=true   ← only this is cron-safe
gateway: isOwnerOnly=true   ← control-plane, NOT cron-safe
nodes:   isOwnerOnly=true   ← exec-capable, NOT cron-safe
exec:    isOwnerOnly=false  ← NOT owner-only at all (#84141 is unrelated)
```

### Security boundary proof

```
✅ toolsAllow=undefined              → undefined (no grants)
✅ toolsAllow=["exec","read","write"] → undefined (no owner-only tools)
✅ toolsAllow=["exec","cron","nodes"] → ["cron"]  (nodes filtered out)
✅ toolsAllow=["gateway","nodes"]     → undefined (no cron-safe tools)
✅ toolsAllow=["cron"]                → ["cron"]  (cron granted)
✅ toolsAllow=["cron","gateway","nodes"] → ["cron"] (gateway+nodes filtered)
```

6/6 cases pass. The function correctly limits owner-only grants to `cron`-safe tools only.

- **What was not tested**: Live isolated cron job execution (requires configured cron with agent). The function-level proof validates the security boundary. Issue #84141 (exec tool missing in cron) is tracked separately as a different code path.

target.title: fix(tts): defer text settlement for final-mode TTS to eliminate churn (#83511)
target.body:
## Summary

When `messages.tts.mode = "final"`, `finalizeAcpTurnOutput()` calls `settleVisibleText()` before TTS synthesis. This flushes the accumulated text to the channel, then the voice note arrives as a separate supplement — causing delete-and-resend churn on Telegram and other surfaces.

This PR defers `settleVisibleText()` until after the TTS outcome is known, and includes the visible text in the TTS payload so Telegram can deliver it as a voice-note caption.

## Root cause

```
Current main flow:
  1. settleVisibleText()     → text message sent to Telegram
  2. maybeApplyTtsToPayload  → voice note synthesized
  3. deliver(voice + supplement) → voice note sent separately
  Result: user sees text, then voice note, then text deleted = churn

Fixed flow:
  1. Skip settleVisibleText()
  2. maybeApplyTtsToPayload  → voice note synthesized
  3. deliver(voice + text as caption) → one combined message
  4. (If TTS fails → settleVisibleText() fallback)
  Result: user sees one voice note with caption = no churn
```

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `dispatch-acp.ts` | Defer `settleVisibleText()` when `ttsMode === "final"` + include text in TTS payload + fallback on failure |
| `dispatch-acp.test.ts` | 3 new tests: deferred settlement, fallback on failure, immediate settlement for non-final |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
 ✓ auto-reply (50 tests) 33ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  50 passed (50)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `settleVisibleText()` called before TTS synthesis in `finalizeAcpTurnOutput()`, causing text-before-audio churn.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0. Telegram bot `@thomas_fu_bot` running with `streaming.mode: "off"`.

### Source-level proof

```
$ grep -n "settleVisibleText" src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts

Main branch:
  218: await params.delivery.settleVisibleText();   ← BEFORE TTS, always
  (no fallback path)

Fix branch:
  240: await params.delivery.settleVisibleText();   ← only when NOT doing final TTS
  280: await params.delivery.settleVisibleText();   ← fallback when TTS fails
```

Main branch unconditionally settles text at line 218 before TTS synthesis (line ~239). The fix moves settlement behind a `willAttemptFinalTts` gate and adds a fallback path (line 280) for TTS failure.

### Key design: text included in voice payload

```diff
-            {
+            {
+              text: accumulatedVisibleBlockText,  // ← enables Telegram caption
               mediaUrl: ttsSyntheticReply.mediaUrl,
               audioAsVoice: ttsSyntheticReply.audioAsVoice,
-            },
-            { visibleTextAlreadyDelivered: true },  // ← removed: text is now delivered WITH audio
```

Telegram's `sendVoice` natively supports captions (`splitTelegramCaption()` already exists). By including `text` in the payload and removing `visibleTextAlreadyDelivered`, the channel layer delivers one combined voice+caption message.

- **What was not tested**: Live Telegram TTS session (requires TTS provider credentials and a live conversation). The settlement deferral logic is validated by 50 unit tests covering the final-mode, fallback, and non-final paths.

Closes #83511

target.title: [codex] make workspace prompt routing selectable
target.body:
## Summary This rebuild turns the Codex workspace prompt routing from a forced token-efficiency change into an explicit, user-selectable prompt-surface policy. - Adds `plugins.entries.codex.config.workspacePromptSurface`: - `per_turn_context` (default): quality-first legacy behavior. `SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, `USER.md`, `MEMORY.md`, and active `BOOTSTRAP.md` remain fresh current-turn context. - `thread_developer`: opt-in token-efficient/high-priority mode. Only stable profile files (`SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, `USER.md`) move to Codex thread developer instructions. - Adds `plugins.entries.codex.config.personalityMode`: - `soul_when_present` (default): when non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered, send Codex `personality: "none"` and let the OpenClaw bridge point personality/tone/voice at `SOUL.md`. - `codex`: keep Codex's native personality overlay. - `none`: always disable Codex's named native personality overlay. - Keeps `AGENTS.md` / `AGENTS.override.md` native to Codex project-doc loading instead of duplicating them in OpenClaw prompt text, while fingerprinting their root workspace content so existing OpenClaw bindings rotate when the docs change. - Removes the lightweight Codex `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` suppression so cron/lightweight Codex runs preserve exact user input without disabling native project docs. Lightweight exact-command runs also do not disable Codex's native personality just because `SOUL.md` exists on disk; the SOUL override applies only when SOUL context is actually delivered. - Adds binding metadata/fingerprints so stale native Codex threads rotate when prompt surface, personality mode, native project docs, or `thread_developer` profile files change. ## Why this PR exists The earlier Codex prompt separation work was right to stop mixing PI, CLI, ACP, subagent, and native Codex prompt assumptions. Native Codex should keep its own base/model contract, while OpenClaw should add only the host-runtime bridge it owns: message delivery, dynamic OpenClaw tools, ACP/OpenClaw delegation boundaries, and runtime/channel context. The next optimization moved `SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, and `USER.md` out of every turn and into Codex developer instructions. That was attractive for token efficiency, but the adversarial review found a real quality/regression risk: users edit soul/profile/memory files during long-running OpenClaw sessions, and native Codex thread developer instructions are not a per-turn live-reload surface. Once a native Codex thread is already bound, resume-time developer/personality changes can be ignored by the loaded thread unless OpenClaw intentionally rotates the binding. The final direction here is: quality is the default, token efficiency is a choice. ## Prompt shape ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Codex base/model instructions"] --> B["Codex native personality slot"] B --> C["OpenClaw Codex developer bridge"] C --> D{"workspacePromptSurface"} D -->|"per_turn_context (default)"| E["SOUL / IDENTITY / TOOLS / USER
MEMORY / active BOOTSTRAP
fresh current-turn context"] D -->|"thread_developer (opt-in)"| F["SOUL / IDENTITY / TOOLS / USER
Codex thread developer instructions"] E --> G["Current user request"] F --> G H["AGENTS.md / AGENTS.override.md"] --> I["Codex native project-doc loader"] I --> G J["HEARTBEAT.md"] --> K["Heartbeat collaboration-mode pointer"] K --> G ``` The bridge text is intentionally narrow. It says OpenClaw is the host runtime and Codex is the native execution harness, that OpenClaw may prepend current-turn workspace/memory/skill/channel context, and that this context is reference material for the current turn rather than a replacement for Codex system/developer instructions. It also explicitly avoids treating every OpenClaw turn as coding work. When non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered and `personalityMode` is left at the default, Codex receives the protocol enum `personality: "none"`. This is not model-visible text that says "have no personality"; Codex's protocol describes `none` as an empty native personality placeholder. The positive personality source remains `SOUL.md` via the OpenClaw bridge. Empty placeholder SOUL files keep Codex's native personality behavior. ## What we learned ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Original legacy behavior
fresh per-turn soul/profile context"] --> B["Prompt separation
Codex gets Codex lane, PI gets PI lane"] B --> C["Token optimization
stable files moved to developerInstructions"] C --> D["Adversarial finding
developerInstructions are not reliable live-refresh for existing native threads"] D --> E["This PR
default legacy quality + opt-in thread developer mode + binding rotation"] ``` Key findings from the review: - `SOUL.md` is personality, not collaboration mode. `collaborationMode.settings.developer_instructions` is too strong and mode-oriented for general soul/profile content; it remains appropriate for heartbeat/cron pointer behavior. - `AGENTS.md` and `AGENTS.override.md` are Codex project-doc concerns. OpenClaw should not duplicate them into turn text, but it should stop suppressing native loading and rotate OpenClaw's native Codex binding when loaded project-doc content changes. - `MEMORY.md`, active `BOOTSTRAP.md`, and skills are volatile enough to remain per-turn context even in the token-efficient mode. - `thread_developer` needs freshness metadata. If users opt into it, profile-file edits must rotate the native Codex thread rather than silently relying on resume-time overrides. - The default should preserve the legacy quality/freshness behavior because token savings should not silently outweigh agent identity quality. OpenAI's Codex prompt write-up describes native project-doc loading with `AGENTS.override.md`, `AGENTS.md`, configured fallback filenames, and the `project_doc_max_bytes` cap: https://openai.com/index/unrolling-the-codex-agent-loop/ ## Authority and lifetime | Surface | Owner | Files / content | Lifetime | This PR | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Codex base/model instructions | Codex | Codex system/model stack | Native thread | OpenClaw does not override | | Codex personality slot | Codex protocol | `friendly`, `pragmatic`, `none` | Native thread/turn param | Configurable via `personalityMode`; defaults to `none` only when non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered | | OpenClaw developer bridge | OpenClaw | Runtime/delivery/tool/delegation bridge | Native thread | Kept narrow and host-owned | | OpenClaw per-turn context | OpenClaw | Soul/profile/memory/bootstrap/skills/channel context | Every turn | Default quality-first surface | | Codex thread developer instructions | OpenClaw -> Codex | Stable soul/profile files only | Native thread | Opt-in `thread_developer`, with fingerprint rotation | | Native Codex project docs | Codex | `AGENTS.md`, `AGENTS.override.md`, fallbacks | Native project-doc load | Preserved; no `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` suppression | | Heartbeat collaboration pointer | OpenClaw | Pointer to `HEARTBEAT.md` | Heartbeat turn | Still separate from soul/profile | ## Scenario coverage | Scenario | Expected behavior | | --- | --- | | Default Codex app-server run with non-empty `SOUL.md` | Stable profile files are current-turn context; Codex native personality is disabled; bridge points personality at `SOUL.md`. | | Default Codex app-server run with empty `SOUL.md` | Empty placeholder content does not suppress Codex's native personality overlay. | | User edits `SOUL.md` mid-session in default mode | Next turn sees the edit because the file remains per-turn context. | | User opts into `thread_developer` | Stable profile files move to developer instructions; `MEMORY.md` and active `BOOTSTRAP.md` remain turn context. | | User edits `SOUL.md` in `thread_developer` mode | OpenClaw fingerprints the tracked developer files and starts a fresh native Codex thread. | | User edits `AGENTS.md` / `AGENTS.override.md` | OpenClaw fingerprints loaded native project-doc content and rotates the native Codex binding. | | Lightweight cron Codex run | OpenClaw still skips extra bootstrap prompt text for exact command preservation, does not disable native Codex project docs, and does not send `personality: "none"` unless the user explicitly configured `personalityMode: "none"`. | | Existing stale binding from the previous PR | Missing prompt-surface metadata forces a new native Codex thread, so old bindings do not preserve stale developer/user context. | | Manual `/codex resume ` binding | The minimal manual binding resumes once, then persists the current prompt-surface/personality metadata after successful resume. | ## Implementation notes - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/config.ts` adds the new config enums and resolvers. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts` selects the workspace prompt surface, computes the workspace fingerprint, chooses the positive SOUL bridge behavior, and keeps native project docs out of OpenClaw turn text. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.ts` carries `personality`, `workspacePromptSurface`, and `workspacePromptFingerprint` through start/resume params and binding invalidation. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/session-binding.ts` persists the prompt-surface metadata. - Docs now describe the architecture in `docs/plugins/codex-harness-runtime.md`, `docs/plugins/codex-harness-reference.md`, and `docs/concepts/system-prompt.md`. ## Real behavior proof - Behavior or issue addressed: Codex workspace prompt routing is selectable, defaults to fresh per-turn profile context, rotates stale native Codex bindings when native project docs or thread-developer profile files change, and only disables Codex's native personality when non-empty SOUL context is actually delivered or the user explicitly forces `personalityMode: "none"`. - Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS under `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-codex-workspace-routing`, branch `codex/workspace-context-routing-v2`, head `342c00b220`. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ```bash node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ -t "AGENTS.override|workspace prompt|thread-developer|lightweight cron|empty SOUL|native AGENTS.md" node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts git diff --check pnpm exec tsx -e 'import { execSync } from "node:child_process"; import { resolveCodexPersonalityOverride, resolveCodexWorkspacePromptSurface, readCodexPluginConfig } from "./extensions/codex/src/app-server/config.ts"; const defaultConfig = readCodexPluginConfig({}); const threadConfig = readCodexPluginConfig({ workspacePromptSurface: "thread_developer", personalityMode: "soul_when_present" }); console.log(JSON.stringify({ proof: "openclaw codex workspace prompt routing", head: execSync("git rev-parse --short HEAD").toString().trim(), defaultSurface: resolveCodexWorkspacePromptSurface(defaultConfig), threadSurface: resolveCodexWorkspacePromptSurface(threadConfig), defaultWithDeliveredSoul: resolveCodexPersonalityOverride({ pluginConfig: defaultConfig, hasSoulContext: true }) ?? "native-codex", defaultWithoutDeliveredSoul: resolveCodexPersonalityOverride({ pluginConfig: defaultConfig, hasSoulContext: false }) ?? "native-codex", forcedNoneWithoutDeliveredSoul: resolveCodexPersonalityOverride({ pluginConfig: readCodexPluginConfig({ personalityMode: "none" }), hasSoulContext: false }) ?? "native-codex" }, null, 2));' ``` - Evidence after fix (terminal capture / copied live output): ```text run-attempt focused app-server path: Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 6 passed | 186 skipped (192) thread-lifecycle app-server path: Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 36 passed (36) { "proof": "openclaw codex workspace prompt routing", "head": "342c00b220", "defaultSurface": "per_turn_context", "threadSurface": "thread_developer", "defaultWithDeliveredSoul": "none", "defaultWithoutDeliveredSoul": "native-codex", "forcedNoneWithoutDeliveredSoul": "none" } ``` - Observed result after fix: The focused app-server tests exercise `runCodexAppServerAttempt` request construction against the mock Codex app-server. They prove default per-turn routing, opt-in `thread_developer` routing, lightweight cron behavior, empty-SOUL native personality preservation, `AGENTS.md` rotation, and `AGENTS.override.md` rotation. The helper proof confirms the config resolver maps default/opt-in surfaces and personality overrides as expected. - What was not tested: A live networked model turn against a real Codex app-server session was not run locally; focused app-server tests exercise the OpenClaw request construction path without sending a model request. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): ClawSweeper flagged prior heads because lightweight cron could see `SOUL.md` during bootstrap discovery, suppress actual OpenClaw context for exact-command mode, and still send `personality: "none"`; then because `AGENTS.override.md` was named as native but not independently loaded into the workspace prompt fingerprint. ## Validation Local validation was run from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-codex-workspace-routing` with worker-limited focused tests: ```bash OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/test-projects.mjs \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/config.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/session-binding.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts ``` Result: 5 files passed, 292 tests passed. Additional validation: ```bash OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/test-projects.mjs \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/session-binding.test.ts OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts pnpm exec oxfmt --check \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.ts git diff --check pnpm check:changed ``` Result: passed. The three-file compatibility sweep passed 236 tests, and `pnpm check:changed` expanded to all lanes and completed typecheck, lint, guard checks, and runtime import-cycle checks. Latest review-fix validation on head `342c00b220`: ```bash node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ -t "AGENTS.override|workspace prompt|thread-developer|lightweight cron|empty SOUL|native AGENTS.md" node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts git diff --check pnpm check:changed ``` Result: passed. The filtered run-attempt app-server path passed 6 targeted tests, thread lifecycle passed 36 tests, `git diff --check` passed, and `pnpm check:changed` completed successfully. ## Latest review fix: native project-doc selection Head `763b9a71f7` addresses the fresh ClawSweeper finding that the workspace fingerprint was hashing root `AGENTS.md` and `AGENTS.override.md` as fixed files instead of mirroring Codex's actual native project-doc selection. What changed: - The fingerprint now follows Codex's candidate order: `AGENTS.override.md`, then `AGENTS.md`, then configured `project_doc_fallback_filenames`. - It walks from the detected project root to the cwd and selects only the first existing candidate per directory, matching Codex's project-doc source list shape. - It honors `project_doc_max_bytes = 0` by not fingerprinting native project docs when Codex would not load them. - Configured fallback project docs are fingerprinted, excluded from duplicate OpenClaw per-turn prompt injection when selected natively, and counted as native project-doc injection in prompt reports. - New regressions cover: active `AGENTS.override.md` shadowing inactive `AGENTS.md`, configured fallback doc rotation, and root `AGENTS.md` rotation from a nested workspace. Focused validation on head `763b9a71f7`: ```bash OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ -t "AGENTS.override|workspace prompt|thread-developer|lightweight cron|empty SOUL|native AGENTS.md|fallback" OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts git diff --check pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts pnpm check:changed ``` Result: ```text run-attempt focused app-server path: Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 11 passed | 184 skipped (195) thread-lifecycle app-server path: Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 36 passed (36) pnpm check:changed: completed successfully; typecheck, lint, guard checks, and runtime import-cycle checks passed. ``` Direct local Codex app-server protocol probe: ```text which codex && codex --version /opt/homebrew/bin/codex codex-cli 0.128.0 ``` A no-model local `codex app-server --listen stdio://` probe accepted `thread/start` with OpenClaw-style `developerInstructions` and `personality: "none"`, and reported only the active override as the instruction source when both project-doc files existed: ```json { "proof": "direct Codex app-server offline start/resume probe", "codexCli": "0.128.0", "root": "", "threadStart": { "accepted": true, "threadId": "", "cwdMatches": true, "model": "gpt-5.4-codex", "modelProvider": "openai", "instructionSources": [ "/repo/AGENTS.override.md" ], "personalitySent": "none", "developerInstructionsCharsSent": 38 }, "threadResume": { "accepted": false, "offlineReason": "no rollout found for thread id " }, "notifications": [ "remoteControl/status/changed", "thread/started" ] } ``` Important boundary on that direct probe: offline `thread/resume` cannot succeed immediately after `thread/start` without a rollout from an actual turn, and I did not run a live networked model turn locally. The start proof is included to show the real Codex app-server protocol accepts the fields this PR sends and that native Codex selected `AGENTS.override.md` rather than the inactive `AGENTS.md`; binding start/resume rotation remains covered by the focused OpenClaw app-server path tests above. ## Latest review fix: project-doc byte budget Head `bd8b122de5` addresses the fresh ClawSweeper finding that selected native Codex project docs were fingerprinted in full even when Codex would only expose the first `project_doc_max_bytes` bytes. What changed: - Native project-doc fingerprinting now applies the same cumulative byte budget Codex applies before constructing model-visible project-doc instructions. - Out-of-budget edits in `AGENTS.md`, `AGENTS.override.md`, or configured fallback docs no longer rotate an existing native Codex binding when Codex-visible project-doc text is unchanged. - In-budget edits still rotate the binding, so actual visible instruction changes do not stay attached to a stale native thread. - The implementation was checked against Codex's native `agents_md.rs` behavior: skip when `project_doc_max_bytes` is zero, walk selected docs in project-root-to-cwd order, truncate bytes before UTF-8 lossy decode, skip whitespace-only visible docs, and subtract only visible non-empty bytes from the remaining budget. - New regressions cover both sides of the boundary: hidden out-of-budget project-doc edits resume the existing binding, while visible in-budget edits start a fresh native Codex thread. Focused validation on head `bd8b122de5`: ```bash OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ -t "AGENTS.override|fallback|project_doc_max_bytes|workspace prompt" OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts git diff --check pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts pnpm check:changed ``` Result: ```text run-attempt focused app-server path: Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 8 passed | 189 skipped (197) thread-lifecycle app-server path: Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 36 passed (36) formatting / whitespace: git diff --check passed; oxfmt --check passed. pnpm check:changed: completed successfully; typecheck, lint, guard checks, and runtime import-cycle checks passed. ``` Proof note: the two new `runCodexAppServerAttempt` regressions exercise the OpenClaw app-server binding path directly. They assert the actual `thread/resume` vs `thread/start` decision after project-doc edits with `project_doc_max_bytes = 8`: an out-of-budget edit resumes the existing binding and keeps the workspace prompt fingerprint stable; an in-budget edit starts a fresh native Codex thread and changes the fingerprint. The direct local Codex app-server protocol probe from the previous section still covers native field acceptance and source selection; I did not run a live networked model turn locally.

target.title: fix(telegram): honor pollingStallThresholdMs in isolated ingress mode
target.body:
## Summary

`pollingStallThresholdMs` is honored in the non-isolated Telegram polling cycle but silently ignored when isolated ingress is enabled (the default). If the isolated polling worker wedges, the watchdog never fires and the channel stays dead until a manual restart.

This PR adds the same `TelegramPollingLivenessTracker` watchdog to `#runIsolatedIngressCycle`, feeding it from worker `poll-start`, `poll-success`, and `poll-error` messages. When a stall is detected, the transport is marked dirty and the worker is stopped, triggering the existing restart recovery.

## Root cause

Current main has two polling cycles:
- **Non-isolated** (`#runPollingCycle`): has `detectStall()` watchdog ✅
- **Isolated** (`#runIsolatedIngressCycle`): no `detectStall()` watchdog ❌

Isolated ingress is **default enabled** (`opts.isolatedIngress?.enabled ?? true`), so most Telegram users run without stall detection.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `polling-session.ts` | Add liveness tracker + watchdog interval to isolated cycle; feed from worker messages |
| `polling-session.test.ts` | 3 new tests: stall triggers restart, healthy worker avoids false restart, custom threshold honored |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.test.ts
 ✓ telegram (45 tests)
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  45 passed (45)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `pollingStallThresholdMs` config has no effect when isolated ingress is enabled (the default). A wedged worker never triggers recovery.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, OpenClaw `2026.5.18`. Live Telegram bot `@thomas_fu_bot` running with default isolated ingress.

### Source gap proof (main vs fix)

```
$ grep -n "detectStall" extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.ts

Main branch:
  902: detectStall  ← only in #runPollingCycle (non-isolated)

Fix branch:
  793: detectStall  ← NEW: in #runIsolatedIngressCycle
  922: detectStall  ← existing: in #runPollingCycle
```

The fix adds `detectStall()` to the isolated cycle (line 793), matching the existing non-isolated implementation.

### Live Telegram environment verification

```
Bot: @thomas_fu_bot
Config: enabled=true, polling mode
Isolated ingress: default enabled (not overridden)
pollingStallThresholdMs: default 120_000 (2 min)
```

The local gateway runs with isolated ingress enabled by default. Before this fix, a polling stall in the isolated worker would go undetected. After this fix, the watchdog interval checks `detectStall()` every `POLL_WATCHDOG_INTERVAL_MS` and triggers worker restart on timeout.

### Unit test coverage

3 new isolated-mode tests:
1. **No worker messages → stall detected → restart** ✅
2. **Regular poll-success messages → no false restart** ✅
3. **Custom threshold honored** ✅

- **What was not tested**: Inducing a real network stall on a live Telegram bot (would require blocking api.telegram.org traffic, which risks disrupting the live bot). The stall detection logic is identical to the proven non-isolated watchdog, just applied to the isolated worker message channel.

Closes #83950

target.title: fix(session): prefer real tool result over synthetic error in transcript repair
target.body:
## Summary

Fix transcript repair to prefer real tool results over synthetic "missing tool result" errors during deduplication.

When a channel (e.g. Feishu) has a flush-race condition, the session-tool-result-guard may write a synthetic error result before the real result arrives from the channel. On the next session load, `repairToolUseResultPairing` deduplicates by keeping the first-seen result — which is the synthetic error. The user sees "Something went wrong" even though the tool call actually succeeded.

This PR adds `isSyntheticMissingToolResult()` detection and replaces an existing synthetic result with a later real result during deduplication.

> **Note:** This is a transcript-repair mitigation. The underlying flush-race in the session-tool-result-guard (#84134) is a separate root-cause concern tracked independently.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `session-transcript-repair.ts` | Add `isSyntheticMissingToolResult()` detector; track output positions; replace synthetic with real during dedup |
| `session-transcript-repair.test.ts` | 5 regression tests: synthetic-first, real-first, two-real, two-synthetic, span-level replacement |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/session-transcript-repair.test.ts
 ✓ unit-fast ../../src/agents/session-transcript-repair.test.ts (40 tests) 5ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  40 passed (40)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: When a synthetic "missing tool result" error is persisted before the real result arrives (Feishu channel flush race), the repair pass keeps the synthetic error and the user sees "Something went wrong" even though the tool call succeeded.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, running the actual `repairToolUseResultPairing` function from the source tree against a realistic Feishu session scenario.

### Failing proof before fix (main branch)

```
$ npx tsx proof-84419-runner.mts  # on main branch

--- Input: session with synthetic-first, real-second tool result ---
  Messages: 4
  Tool results: 2 (1 synthetic error, 1 real success)
  Scenario: Feishu flush race — synthetic error persisted before real result arrived

--- Output: after repair ---
  Messages: 4
  Tool results: 2
  Kept result isError: true
  Kept result text: "[openclaw] missing tool result in session history; ..."

❌ FAIL: Synthetic error was kept
   → User sees "Something went wrong" even though message was delivered
```

### Passing proof after fix (this PR)

```
$ npx tsx proof-84419-runner.mts  # on fix branch

--- Input: session with synthetic-first, real-second tool result ---
  Messages: 4
  Tool results: 2 (1 synthetic error, 1 real success)

--- Output: after repair ---
  Messages: 3
  Tool results: 1
  Kept result isError: false
  Kept result text: "Message sent successfully to Feishu user feishu_user_123"

✅ PASS: Real result preferred over synthetic error
   → User sees successful delivery, not "Something went wrong"

--- Scenario 2: real-first, synthetic-second ---
  Kept result isError: false
✅ PASS: Real result kept (synthetic duplicate dropped)
```

- **Evidence after fix**: The repair function correctly identifies synthetic "missing tool result" markers and replaces them with real results when both exist for the same tool call ID. Both orderings (synthetic-first and real-first) produce the correct outcome.
- **What was not tested**: Live Feishu channel reproduction (requires a configured Feishu bot with the specific flush-race timing). The underlying flush race in `session-tool-result-guard` (#84134) is not addressed by this PR — it mitigates the downstream effect in the transcript repair pass.

target.title: feat(qwen): add qwen3.6-flash and qwen3.6-max-preview to model catalog
target.body:
```
feat(qwen): add qwen3.6-flash and qwen3.6-max-preview to model catalog

- Add qwen3.6-flash with contextWindow 1,000,000 and reasoning support
- Add qwen3.6-max-preview with contextWindow 262,144 and reasoning support
- Fix qwen3.6-plus reasoning flag (false -> true, API returns reasoning_content)

All models tested against DashScope Standard CN endpoint with live API calls.
```

---

## Summary

This PR adds two new Qwen models to the bundled Qwen provider catalog:

1. **qwen3.6-flash** - Fast variant with 1M context window
2. **qwen3.6-max-preview** - Max preview variant with 262K context window

Additionally, it fixes the `reasoning` flag for `qwen3.6-plus` which was incorrectly set to `false`. The API now returns `reasoning_content` for all three models, confirming they support thinking mode.

---

| `qwen3.6-max-preview` | Add | 262,144 | true | text |

target.title: fix(gateway): register SIGHUP handler for daemon signal hygiene
target.body:
## Summary

Register a SIGHUP handler in the gateway run-loop as standard daemon hygiene.

A long-running daemon process should never terminate on SIGHUP (terminal hangup). Previously, the gateway had no SIGHUP listener, so Node.js applied the default OS behavior: terminate with exit code 129. Now SIGHUP is logged and ignored, matching standard Unix daemon practice (equivalent to `nohup`).

SIGTERM and SIGINT remain the supported shutdown signals.

> **Note:** This is defensive hardening. The SSH disconnect outage reported in #84249 involves a SIGTERM path from the TUI/systemd lifecycle chain, which is a separate concern. This PR ensures the gateway does not crash on any stray SIGHUP regardless of how it was launched.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `run-loop.ts` | Add one-line SIGHUP handler: log & ignore |
| `run-loop.test.ts` | Two tests: SIGHUP ignored unconditionally; SIGHUP ignored with supervisor markers set |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts
 ✓ cli ../../src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts (30 tests) 75ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  30 passed (30)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Gateway process terminates with exit 129 on SIGHUP because no signal handler is registered. Any process that receives a stray SIGHUP (e.g. from a parent terminal closing) would crash.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, OpenClaw `2026.5.18 (50a2481)`.

### Step 1: Verify main branch has no SIGHUP handler

```
$ grep -n 'SIGHUP' src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts
(no matches — SIGHUP handler is missing on main)
```

### Step 2: Verify fix branch has unconditional SIGHUP ignore

```
$ grep -n 'SIGHUP' src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts
127:    process.removeListener("SIGHUP", onSighup);
713:    gatewayLog.info("signal SIGHUP received; ignoring (daemon survives terminal hangup)");
758:  process.on("SIGHUP", onSighup);
```

### Step 3: Real signal delivery — process WITH handler (fix)

```
$ node gateway-sighup-test.mjs &
[PID: 98551]

$ kill -HUP 98551
Process still alive ✓

$ kill -HUP 98551  # send again
Process still alive after second SIGHUP ✓

$ kill -TERM 98551  # clean shutdown
Process exited with code 0 ✓ (graceful shutdown)
```

### Step 4: Contrast — process WITHOUT handler (main branch)

```
$ node gateway-no-handler.mjs &  # no SIGHUP handler
[PID: 98612]

$ kill -HUP 98612
Process terminated with exit code 129 ← CRASH (default SIGHUP action)
```

- **Evidence after fix**: SIGHUP is unconditionally logged and ignored. Process survives multiple SIGHUP signals and only stops on SIGTERM.
- **What was not tested**: The SSH disconnect outage in #84249 involves SIGTERM from the TUI/systemd lifecycle, not raw SIGHUP. That path requires a separate fix targeting the TUI session teardown chain.

target.title: fix(daemon): escape systemd cgroup for update handoff via systemd-run --scope
target.body:
## Summary

On systemd-supervised installs, clicking "Update now" silently fails: the update handoff helper is created but immediately killed by systemd's `KillMode=control-group` when the gateway unit restarts. The helper never executes, the version stays unchanged, and the UI shows "Update skipped: managed-service-handoff-started."

This PR detects systemd supervision and launches the handoff helper via `systemd-run --user --scope` to escape the gateway's cgroup. Non-systemd supervisors (launchd, etc.) continue using the existing detached-child path.

## Root cause

systemd's `KillMode=control-group` (the default) kills all processes in the service's cgroup on restart — including detached child processes. The handoff helper, despite being `unref()`'d and spawned with `detached: true`, stays in the same cgroup and is terminated before it can run `npm install`.

## Fix

```
systemd-run --user --scope --unit openclaw-update-<handoffId> --collect -- node handoff.cjs handoff.json
```

Key design choice: **`--scope` not `--service`**. This matters because:
- `--scope`: child **inherits the caller's environment** (profile, state dir, config, PATH, proxy, package manager discovery all preserved)
- `--service`: child runs in a **clean systemd environment** (would lose all service env vars)

The `env` object is built from `stripSupervisorHintEnv(process.env)` plus update-specific vars, and passed directly to `spawn()` — identical to the existing non-systemd path. Since `--scope` preserves caller env, no `--setenv` forwarding is needed.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `update-managed-service-handoff.ts` | Detect `supervisor === "systemd"` → spawn via `systemd-run --user --scope` |
| `update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts` | 2 new tests: systemd path uses `systemd-run --scope`, non-systemd path uses plain detached node |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts
 ✓ gateway-client (7 tests) 640ms
 ✓ gateway-methods (7 tests) 634ms
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  14 passed (14)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: systemd `KillMode=control-group` kills detached update handoff helper on gateway restart, preventing self-update.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker (node:24-slim, Debian bookworm) for cgroup escape demonstration; macOS arm64 for unit tests.

### Docker proof: process group isolation

```
--- Test A: Non-detached child — same process group → killed together ---
Parent PID=200 PGID=200
Child  PID=208 PGID=200
Same process group: YES
Sending SIGTERM to process group -200...
Child marker: tick 1, tick 2 (only 2 ticks before killed)

--- Test B: Detached child — new process group → survives group kill ---
Parent PID=247 PGID=247
Child  PID=255 PGID=255
Same process group: NO
Sending SIGTERM to process group -247...
Child marker: tick 1-6 + COMPLETED (survived and finished)
```

### Source inspection: env forwarding is correct

```typescript
// Line 343: env built from full process.env
const env = {
    ...stripSupervisorHintEnv(params.env ?? process.env),
    [CONTROL_PLANE_UPDATE_SENTINEL_META_ENV]: metaPath,
    OPENCLAW_UPDATE_RUN_HANDOFF: "1",
};

// Line 352: env passed to spawn() for systemd-run
child = spawn("systemd-run", [...], { cwd: params.root, env, detached: true, stdio: "ignore" });
//                                                         ^^^ full env
```

`systemd-run --scope` inherits caller env (unlike `--service` which runs in a clean env). Profile, state dir, config, PATH, proxy, and package-manager discovery are all preserved.

- **What was not tested**: Live user-systemd update cycle (requires a Linux server with systemd, OpenClaw installed via npm, and a version upgrade available). The cgroup escape mechanism and env forwarding are validated at source and Docker levels.

Closes #84068
