target.title: fix(gateway): include MCP tools in effective inventory
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the Agent Tools dashboard / `tools.effective` inventory originally reported only static OpenClaw tools, so configured bundled MCP tools could be usable at runtime but absent from the session-effective inventory.
- Solution: split the Gateway surface so `tools.effective` is a read-only peek projection with explicit MCP notices, and add `tools.effective.refresh` as an `operator.write` method that performs live MCP discovery/materialization on demand.
- What changed: effective inventory projection supports `source: "mcp"`; session MCP runtimes expose peek-only catalog metadata; the Gateway read path no longer cold-starts MCP transports; the refresh path materializes MCP tools through the existing runtime/materializer/final-policy path; Control UI adds an explicit **Refresh Available Tools** action; SDK/docs/protocol/tests cover the new split.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no provider transport changes, no Tool Search/Code Mode raw provider-body changes, no auto-reply `/tools` command changes, no install-package-dir changes, no broad shared-runtime refactor, and no claim that #80909 or the reporter-specific SSE raw provider-body path is fixed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #: N/A (intentionally not closing #76724; this keeps the reporter-specific SSE/runtime-provider-body path out of scope)
- Related / Refs #76724
- Related #70876
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

#76724 reports MCP handshake success while the Agent Tools dashboard stays stuck at the pre-MCP tool count and does not list MCP tools. Runtime code can materialize bundled MCP tools for model runs, but the dashboard inventory used a separate static inventory path. The initial patch made `tools.effective` perform live MCP materialization; after review of the read/security boundary, this update keeps `tools.effective` read-only and moves live discovery behind an explicit refresh method.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `tools.effective` should not cold-start/list MCP transports from a read-scoped dashboard load, while the dashboard must still have an explicit path to discover configured MCP tools and then show the warm catalog.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw Gateway from this branch at `3c7a4e67b6`, QA mock OpenAI provider, real stdio MCP server created by the proof harness, Tool Search disabled, Code Mode disabled.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_REPO_ROOT=/root/repos/openclaw-mcp-tools-effective \
OPENCLAW_LOCAL_HEAD=3c7a4e67b6 \
OPENCLAW_UPSTREAM_COMMIT=110042d840 \
OPENCLAW_RPC_PROOF_SUMMARY=/tmp/openclaw-mcp-tools-effective-d-proof-summary.json \
node --import tsx /tmp/openclaw-mcp-tools-effective-d-proof.ts
```

- Evidence after fix:

Copied live Gateway RPC harness output from this branch:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "proof": "D read/refresh split: cold read peek-only, explicit refresh materializes MCP, warm read peeks catalog",
  "upstreamCommit": "110042d840",
  "localHead": "3c7a4e67b6",
  "cold": {
    "noticeIds": ["mcp-not-yet-connected"],
    "containsMcpTarget": false,
    "runtimeCreated": false,
    "groupSummaries": [
      { "id": "core", "source": "core", "toolCount": 18 },
      { "id": "plugin", "source": "plugin", "toolCount": 2 }
    ]
  },
  "refreshed": {
    "noticeIds": [],
    "containsMcpTarget": true,
    "matchingIds": ["reproProbe__probe_tool"],
    "runtimeCreated": true,
    "catalogToolNames": ["probe_tool"],
    "groupSummaries": [
      { "id": "core", "source": "core", "toolCount": 18 },
      { "id": "plugin", "source": "plugin", "toolCount": 2 },
      { "id": "mcp", "source": "mcp", "toolCount": 1, "sampleIds": ["reproProbe__probe_tool"] }
    ]
  },
  "warm": {
    "noticeIds": [],
    "containsMcpTarget": true,
    "matchingIds": ["reproProbe__probe_tool"],
    "groupSummaries": [
      { "id": "core", "source": "core", "toolCount": 18 },
      { "id": "plugin", "source": "plugin", "toolCount": 2 },
      { "id": "mcp", "source": "mcp", "toolCount": 1, "sampleIds": ["reproProbe__probe_tool"] }
    ]
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: a cold `tools.effective` read returns only core/plugin groups plus `mcp-not-yet-connected` and does not create a session MCP runtime; explicit `tools.effective.refresh` materializes the configured stdio MCP server and returns `reproProbe__probe_tool`; the next `tools.effective` read shows the warm MCP group via peek without another cold materialization.
- What was not tested: the reporter's original Flask SSE server and release version `v2026.5.2`; Tool Search / Code Mode raw provider-body behavior; #80909; full cross-platform/browser E2E. This PR is scoped to the Gateway/dashboard effective-inventory boundary.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): pre-patch proof on current main returned 20 tools and omitted `reproProbe__probe_tool` from `tools.effective` while the runtime provider path could include it.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `tools.effective` called the static `resolveEffectiveToolInventory()` resolver, which builds from `createOpenClawCodingTools()` only. Bundled MCP tools are appended later in the model-run path through the session MCP runtime/materializer, so the dashboard inventory and runtime provider path diverged.
- Boundary correction in this update: live MCP discovery/listing can start configured stdio/remote transports, so it now lives behind explicit `operator.write` `tools.effective.refresh`; the read-scoped method only peeks at an existing matching-fingerprint runtime catalog and otherwise returns a notice.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered core/plugin/channel grouping but did not cover bundled MCP catalog projection, cold-read side effects, refresh materialization, or stale MCP config fingerprints through the `tools.effective` handler/RPC path.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] End-to-end-style Gateway RPC proof
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/gateway/server-methods/tools-effective.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools-effective-inventory.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`
  - `packages/sdk/src/index.test.ts`
  - `packages/sdk/src/index.e2e.test.ts`
  - `ui/src/ui/controllers/agents.test.ts`
  - `ui/src/ui/views/agents-panels-tools-skills.browser.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: cold `tools.effective` does not create/list MCP and returns notices; warm read projects an existing same-fingerprint catalog; fingerprint mismatch returns refresh-needed notice instead of stale MCP tools; explicit `tools.effective.refresh` materializes MCP tools and applies final effective policy; UI uses read on normal load and refresh on explicit action.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug and security-boundary concern live at the seam between static inventory, session MCP runtime, Gateway method scope, SDK, and UI refresh behavior.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none found for bundled MCP in `tools-effective` / `tools-effective-inventory` before this patch.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

The Agent Tools panel now loads without implicitly starting configured MCP transports. If MCP servers are configured but not connected/listed yet, the panel shows an explanatory notice and a **Refresh Available Tools** action. Triggering refresh performs live discovery and lets subsequent reads show the warm MCP tools.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before this PR:
[tools.effective] -> [static createOpenClawCodingTools inventory] -> [core/plugin/channel only]

Updated design:
[tools.effective]         -> [static inventory + peek existing MCP catalog] -> [core/plugin/channel + notices or warm mcp]
[tools.effective.refresh] -> [session MCP runtime materialize] -> [final bundled-tool policy] -> [core/plugin/channel + mcp]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No new capability beyond existing configured MCP materialization; yes, a new explicit Gateway method is added.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? Yes, only from explicit `tools.effective.refresh` or existing run-lifecycle MCP materialization, not from cold `tools.effective` reads.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes, refresh can start/list configured MCP transports; it does not invoke MCP tools.
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: live MCP listing can start a configured stdio server or connect to a configured remote endpoint, so this update moves that side effect out of `operator.read` and into explicit `operator.write` `tools.effective.refresh`. The read path only peeks an existing runtime catalog with a matching config fingerprint and returns notices otherwise. Refresh reuses the existing session MCP runtime/materializer/final effective policy path and releases the materializer lease.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux local dev environment
- Runtime/container: local Node via `node --import tsx`
- Model/provider: QA mock OpenAI provider, `mock-openai/gpt-5.5`
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway WebSocket RPC `sessions.create`, `tools.effective`, `tools.effective.refresh`, then `tools.effective`
- Relevant config (redacted): `tools.profile: "coding"`, `tools.toolSearch: false`, `tools.codeMode.enabled: false`, `mcp.servers.reproProbe` stdio server command `node <tmp>/dummy-mcp-server.mjs`

### Steps

1. Start QA mock provider and local Gateway from this branch.
2. Create a session with `sessions.create`.
3. Call cold `tools.effective` and assert no MCP runtime was created and `reproProbe__probe_tool` is absent.
4. Call `tools.effective.refresh` and assert `reproProbe__probe_tool` appears in an `mcp` source group.
5. Call warm `tools.effective` and assert the MCP group is projected via peek.

### Expected

- Cold read: no MCP cold-start/listing; notice returned.
- Refresh: MCP listed/materialized through explicit write-scoped method.
- Warm read: MCP group visible through existing catalog peek.

### Actual

- Verified scenarios:

- Risk: users may expect MCP tools to appear immediately on panel load.

target.title: fix(telegram): honor outbound media max bytes
target.body:
Telegram final replies with media above the default cap can fail even when users configured a larger `channels.telegram.mediaMaxMb` limit.

Fixes #46023

## Affected surface

- `extensions/telegram/src/bot-core.ts`
- `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts`
- `extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts`
- `extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.replies.ts`

## Scope

This is a narrow parameter-threading fix. It carries the existing Telegram media byte cap into final and native reply delivery, then reuses `buildOutboundMediaLoadOptions({ maxBytes })` when outbound media is loaded. No new config, policy, or media-limit strategy is introduced.

Approximate scope: 52 inserted lines / 4 removed lines including regression tests.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram final/native replies with media now thread the configured 50 MiB `mediaMaxMb` cap into outbound media loading instead of falling back to the default video cap.
- Real environment tested: Local Linux executor, branch `wolf/telegram-outbound-media-maxbytes`, head `935da229b9`, Node.js `v22.22.2`; real Telegram Bot API delivery through `sendVideo` into Telegram Desktop.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Started a temporary local Node/tsx harness outside the repository.
  2. Imported OpenClaw source `deliverReplies` from `extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.replies.ts`.
  3. Exercised the real path `deliverReplies` -> `deliverMediaReply` -> `loadWebMedia` -> Telegram Bot API `sendVideo`.
  4. Served a valid 35,462,055-byte MP4 from a local HTTP server and ran the before case without `mediaMaxBytes`.
  5. Re-ran the same delivery with `mediaMaxBytes = 52428800`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Telegram Desktop visible proof is attached at https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ed6790f0-d4b3-431d-b333-a2bc43d9e06c and also referenced from https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/83478#issuecomment-4496035844.

  Redacted local live run log:

  ```text
  [server] local_http=started url=<local-http-url>
  [before] start file_size=35462055 maxBytes=<default> url=<local-http-url>
  [before] loadWebMedia input url=<local-http-url> maxBytes=<default>
  [before] result=error error=Media exceeds 16MB limit (got 33.82MB)
  [after] start file_size=35462055 maxBytes=52428800 url=<local-http-url>
  [after] loadWebMedia input url=<local-http-url> maxBytes=52428800
  [telegram] method=sendVideo chat_id=<redacted-chat-id> status=pending
  [telegram] method=sendVideo http_status=200 message_id=2
  [after] result=success delivered=true
  [server] local_http=stopped
  ```
- Observed result after fix: Without `mediaMaxBytes`, the same 35,462,055-byte MP4 fails in OpenClaw media loading with the default 16 MiB video cap. With `mediaMaxBytes = 52428800`, `loadWebMedia` accepts the media, Telegram Bot API `sendVideo` returns HTTP 200, and Telegram Desktop shows the delivered media.
- What was not tested: This live proof covers the Telegram video reply path on a local Linux executor with real Telegram Bot API delivery; macOS/iOS apps and non-Telegram channels are outside this PR.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): The before case in the same live harness failed before Telegram delivery with `Media exceeds 16MB limit (got 33.82MB)`.

Local regression/changed-check output:

```text
$ pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.test.ts
 Test Files  3 passed (3)
      Tests  138 passed (138)

$ pnpm check:changed --base origin/main
check:changed completed changed extension lanes, extension typecheck, extension test typecheck, oxlint, media helper guard, runtime sidecar loader guard, and import cycle check with no failures.

$ git diff --check
<no output>

$ gitleaks protect --staged --redact
no leaks found
```

## Coverage note

ClawSweeper confirmed in issue #46023 that current main still has this gap: Telegram computes `mediaMaxBytes`, but normal final replies and native-command replies do not thread a media cap into `deliverReplies`, `deliverMediaReply`, or `loadWebMedia`. I also checked open PR coverage before starting; no open PR covered #46023, `Telegram mediaMaxMb`, or `deliverReplies mediaMaxBytes`.

target.title: fix(telegram): honor pollingStallThresholdMs in isolated ingress mode
target.body:
## Summary

`pollingStallThresholdMs` is honored in the non-isolated Telegram polling cycle but silently ignored when isolated ingress is enabled (the default). If the isolated polling worker wedges, the watchdog never fires and the channel stays dead until a manual restart.

This PR adds the same `TelegramPollingLivenessTracker` watchdog to `#runIsolatedIngressCycle`, feeding it from worker `poll-start`, `poll-success`, and `poll-error` messages. When a stall is detected, the transport is marked dirty and the worker is stopped, triggering the existing restart recovery.

## Root cause

Current main has two polling cycles:
- **Non-isolated** (`#runPollingCycle`): has `detectStall()` watchdog ✅
- **Isolated** (`#runIsolatedIngressCycle`): no `detectStall()` watchdog ❌

Isolated ingress is **default enabled** (`opts.isolatedIngress?.enabled ?? true`), so most Telegram users run without stall detection.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `polling-session.ts` | Add liveness tracker + watchdog interval to isolated cycle; feed from worker messages |
| `polling-session.test.ts` | 3 new tests: stall triggers restart, healthy worker avoids false restart, custom threshold honored |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.test.ts
 ✓ telegram (45 tests)
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  45 passed (45)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `pollingStallThresholdMs` config has no effect when isolated ingress is enabled (the default). A wedged worker never triggers recovery.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, OpenClaw `2026.5.18`. Live Telegram bot `@thomas_fu_bot` running with default isolated ingress.

### Source gap proof (main vs fix)

```
$ grep -n "detectStall" extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.ts

Main branch:
  902: detectStall  ← only in #runPollingCycle (non-isolated)

Fix branch:
  793: detectStall  ← NEW: in #runIsolatedIngressCycle
  922: detectStall  ← existing: in #runPollingCycle
```

The fix adds `detectStall()` to the isolated cycle (line 793), matching the existing non-isolated implementation.

### Live Telegram environment verification

```
Bot: @thomas_fu_bot
Config: enabled=true, polling mode
Isolated ingress: default enabled (not overridden)
pollingStallThresholdMs: default 120_000 (2 min)
```

The local gateway runs with isolated ingress enabled by default. Before this fix, a polling stall in the isolated worker would go undetected. After this fix, the watchdog interval checks `detectStall()` every `POLL_WATCHDOG_INTERVAL_MS` and triggers worker restart on timeout.

### Unit test coverage

3 new isolated-mode tests:
1. **No worker messages → stall detected → restart** ✅
2. **Regular poll-success messages → no false restart** ✅
3. **Custom threshold honored** ✅

- **What was not tested**: Inducing a real network stall on a live Telegram bot (would require blocking api.telegram.org traffic, which risks disrupting the live bot). The stall detection logic is identical to the proven non-isolated watchdog, just applied to the isolated worker message channel.

Closes #83950

target.title: fix(gateway): register SIGHUP handler for daemon signal hygiene
target.body:
## Summary

Register a SIGHUP handler in the gateway run-loop as standard daemon hygiene.

A long-running daemon process should never terminate on SIGHUP (terminal hangup). Previously, the gateway had no SIGHUP listener, so Node.js applied the default OS behavior: terminate with exit code 129. Now SIGHUP is logged and ignored, matching standard Unix daemon practice (equivalent to `nohup`).

SIGTERM and SIGINT remain the supported shutdown signals.

> **Note:** This is defensive hardening. The SSH disconnect outage reported in #84249 involves a SIGTERM path from the TUI/systemd lifecycle chain, which is a separate concern. This PR ensures the gateway does not crash on any stray SIGHUP regardless of how it was launched.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `run-loop.ts` | Add one-line SIGHUP handler: log & ignore |
| `run-loop.test.ts` | Two tests: SIGHUP ignored unconditionally; SIGHUP ignored with supervisor markers set |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts
 ✓ cli ../../src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts (30 tests) 75ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  30 passed (30)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Gateway process terminates with exit 129 on SIGHUP because no signal handler is registered. Any process that receives a stray SIGHUP (e.g. from a parent terminal closing) would crash.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, OpenClaw `2026.5.18 (50a2481)`.

### Step 1: Verify main branch has no SIGHUP handler

```
$ grep -n 'SIGHUP' src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts
(no matches — SIGHUP handler is missing on main)
```

### Step 2: Verify fix branch has unconditional SIGHUP ignore

```
$ grep -n 'SIGHUP' src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts
127:    process.removeListener("SIGHUP", onSighup);
713:    gatewayLog.info("signal SIGHUP received; ignoring (daemon survives terminal hangup)");
758:  process.on("SIGHUP", onSighup);
```

### Step 3: Real signal delivery — process WITH handler (fix)

```
$ node gateway-sighup-test.mjs &
[PID: 98551]

$ kill -HUP 98551
Process still alive ✓

$ kill -HUP 98551  # send again
Process still alive after second SIGHUP ✓

$ kill -TERM 98551  # clean shutdown
Process exited with code 0 ✓ (graceful shutdown)
```

### Step 4: Contrast — process WITHOUT handler (main branch)

```
$ node gateway-no-handler.mjs &  # no SIGHUP handler
[PID: 98612]

$ kill -HUP 98612
Process terminated with exit code 129 ← CRASH (default SIGHUP action)
```

- **Evidence after fix**: SIGHUP is unconditionally logged and ignored. Process survives multiple SIGHUP signals and only stops on SIGTERM.
- **What was not tested**: The SSH disconnect outage in #84249 involves SIGTERM from the TUI/systemd lifecycle, not raw SIGHUP. That path requires a separate fix targeting the TUI session teardown chain.

target.title: feat(discord): follow configured users in voice
target.body:
## Summary
- add `channels.discord.voice.followUsers` and `followUsersEnabled` so Discord voice can follow configured users into voice channels
- handle join, move, disconnect, startup voice-state checks, allowed-channel restrictions, missed-event reconciliation, and multi-user handoff for followed voice sessions
- bound follow-user reconciliation to sampled guilds under a per-run REST lookup budget
- document the config toggle and regenerate config metadata

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Live Discord voice follow behavior for the new `channels.discord.voice.followUsers` / `followUsersEnabled` feature, including after-fix automatic join/move/leave behavior in a real OpenClaw Discord runtime.

Real environment tested: Jay's live OpenClaw runtime on macOS with the Discord channel enabled, guild `1474893848617423062`, Wilfred2 Discord bot, and `channels.discord.voice.followUsersEnabled: true` with Jay's Discord user configured in `followUsers`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Deployed the rebuilt OpenClaw runtime, restarted the gateway, joined an allowed Discord voice channel as the configured followed user, let the bot auto-join, moved/left the voice channel, verified the bot voice state cleared with `openclaw message voice status --channel discord --guild-id 1474893848617423062 --user-id 1474887155061882890 --json`, and captured the Discord desktop behavior in a screen recording.

Evidence after fix: Browser-viewable MP4 recording: https://github.com/fuller-stack-dev/openclaw/releases/download/proof/pr-84264-discord-voice-follow-20260519-175551/discord-voice-follow-behavior-proof-2026-05-19.mp4 ; original MOV: https://github.com/fuller-stack-dev/openclaw/releases/download/proof/pr-84264-discord-voice-follow-20260519-175551/Screen.Recording.2026-05-19.at.5.55.51.PM.mov ; live status after disconnect returned `DiscordError: Unknown Voice State` for bot user `1474887155061882890`, confirming the bot was no longer in voice.

Observed result after fix: The configured followed user's Discord voice presence caused Wilfred2 to join the voice channel, and after the user disconnected the bot no longer had a Discord voice state. The repeated stuck-session race was also fixed by preserving follow ownership across bot move rebuilds.

What was not tested: Live multi-user handoff with two human Discord users in voice at the same time was not available in this runtime session; that path is covered by the new `hands off to another followed user when the active followed user disconnects` e2e regression test.

## Review fixes
- Multi-user handoff: when the active followed user disconnects or moves out of allowed voice, the manager now moves to another tracked followed user in the guild before leaving.
- Reconciliation bounds: interval/startup reconciliation now samples whole guilds and caps REST work with `FOLLOW_USERS_RECONCILE_MAX_REST_LOOKUPS_PER_RUN`.
- Merge conflicts: merged current `origin/main` and regenerated `docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256`.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-discord.config.ts extensions/discord/src/config-schema.test.ts` — 33 passed

target.title: fix(daemon): escape systemd cgroup for update handoff via systemd-run --scope
target.body:
## Summary

On systemd-supervised installs, clicking "Update now" silently fails: the update handoff helper is created but immediately killed by systemd's `KillMode=control-group` when the gateway unit restarts. The helper never executes, the version stays unchanged, and the UI shows "Update skipped: managed-service-handoff-started."

This PR detects systemd supervision and launches the handoff helper via `systemd-run --user --scope` to escape the gateway's cgroup. Non-systemd supervisors (launchd, etc.) continue using the existing detached-child path.

## Root cause

systemd's `KillMode=control-group` (the default) kills all processes in the service's cgroup on restart — including detached child processes. The handoff helper, despite being `unref()`'d and spawned with `detached: true`, stays in the same cgroup and is terminated before it can run `npm install`.

## Fix

```
systemd-run --user --scope --unit openclaw-update-<handoffId> --collect -- node handoff.cjs handoff.json
```

Key design choice: **`--scope` not `--service`**. This matters because:
- `--scope`: child **inherits the caller's environment** (profile, state dir, config, PATH, proxy, package manager discovery all preserved)
- `--service`: child runs in a **clean systemd environment** (would lose all service env vars)

The `env` object is built from `stripSupervisorHintEnv(process.env)` plus update-specific vars, and passed directly to `spawn()` — identical to the existing non-systemd path. Since `--scope` preserves caller env, no `--setenv` forwarding is needed.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `update-managed-service-handoff.ts` | Detect `supervisor === "systemd"` → spawn via `systemd-run --user --scope` |
| `update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts` | 2 new tests: systemd path uses `systemd-run --scope`, non-systemd path uses plain detached node |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts
 ✓ gateway-client (7 tests) 640ms
 ✓ gateway-methods (7 tests) 634ms
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  14 passed (14)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: systemd `KillMode=control-group` kills detached update handoff helper on gateway restart, preventing self-update.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker (node:24-slim, Debian bookworm) for cgroup escape demonstration; macOS arm64 for unit tests.

### Docker proof: process group isolation

```
--- Test A: Non-detached child — same process group → killed together ---
Parent PID=200 PGID=200
Child  PID=208 PGID=200
Same process group: YES
Sending SIGTERM to process group -200...
Child marker: tick 1, tick 2 (only 2 ticks before killed)

--- Test B: Detached child — new process group → survives group kill ---
Parent PID=247 PGID=247
Child  PID=255 PGID=255
Same process group: NO
Sending SIGTERM to process group -247...
Child marker: tick 1-6 + COMPLETED (survived and finished)
```

### Source inspection: env forwarding is correct

```typescript
// Line 343: env built from full process.env
const env = {
    ...stripSupervisorHintEnv(params.env ?? process.env),
    [CONTROL_PLANE_UPDATE_SENTINEL_META_ENV]: metaPath,
    OPENCLAW_UPDATE_RUN_HANDOFF: "1",
};

// Line 352: env passed to spawn() for systemd-run
child = spawn("systemd-run", [...], { cwd: params.root, env, detached: true, stdio: "ignore" });
//                                                         ^^^ full env
```

`systemd-run --scope` inherits caller env (unlike `--service` which runs in a clean env). Profile, state dir, config, PATH, proxy, and package-manager discovery are all preserved.

- **What was not tested**: Live user-systemd update cycle (requires a Linux server with systemd, OpenClaw installed via npm, and a version upgrade available). The cgroup escape mechanism and env forwarding are validated at source and Docker levels.

Closes #84068

target.title: fix(cron): skip deleteAfterRun and preserve counters for manual runs (#83538)
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw cron run` (manual/debug runs) on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes the job on success — consuming the one-shot and preventing its scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters, interfering with scheduled retry/backoff state.

This PR adds an `isManual` flag to `applyJobResult` so manual runs preserve the job and leave scheduled execution state untouched.

## Root cause

`applyJobResult()` has no concept of manual vs scheduled runs. When a manual run succeeds on a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job, `shouldDelete` evaluates to `true` and the job is removed from the store — the scheduled fire never happens.

Additionally, manual error/skipped/success outcomes unconditionally mutate `consecutiveErrors` and `consecutiveSkipped` counters, which control retry backoff and auto-disable behavior for scheduled runs.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `timer.ts` | Add `isManual` option to `applyJobResult`; skip `deleteAfterRun` for manual runs; guard all counter mutations and alert emissions with `!isManual` |
| `ops.ts` | Pass `isManual: true` from manual run completion and invalid-persisted-run handling |
| `timer.regression.test.ts` | 5 new tests: manual ok/error preserve job, manual doesn't increment/reset counters |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts
 ✓ cron (45 tests) 144ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  45 passed (45)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes it, preventing scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate scheduled retry counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `aa92a4a5b0`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.

### Source-level proof

```
Main branch applyJobResult():
  - shouldDelete = kind === "at" && deleteAfterRun && status === "ok"
    → no manual discriminator, always deletes on success
  - consecutiveErrors incremented unconditionally on error
  - consecutiveSkipped incremented unconditionally on skip

Fix branch:
  - shouldDelete = wouldDelete && !opts?.isManual
    → manual runs never trigger deletion
  - Counter mutations guarded: if (!opts?.isManual) { ... }
    → manual runs don't affect retry/backoff state
```

### Regression test coverage

```
✅ manual ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted
✅ manual error on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted  
✅ scheduled ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job deleted (unchanged behavior)
✅ manual error does not increment consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
✅ manual success does not reset consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
```

- **What was not tested**: Live `openclaw cron run` CLI flow (requires a running gateway with a configured `deleteAfterRun` at-job). The `isManual` flag propagation from `ops.ts` to `applyJobResult` is validated at the unit level.

Closes #83538

target.body:
## Summary
- add `channels.discord.voice.realtime.bootstrapContextFiles` and `bootstrapContextMaxChars` so Discord realtime voice can preload selected profile files
- limit the realtime bootstrap surface to `IDENTITY.md`, `USER.md`, and `SOUL.md`; `AGENTS.md` remains normal agent context and is rejected by config validation
- expose the reusable helper through `openclaw/plugin-sdk/realtime-bootstrap-context`
- document the Discord config and regenerate config/plugin-SDK metadata

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Discord realtime `bidi`/fast-path voice sessions can now start with the same compact identity, user, and persona grounding as the routed OpenClaw agent without injecting broad agent instructions or workspace policy files.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, installed as the active `openclaw` CLI by linking this checkout; managed Gateway running `OpenClaw 2026.5.19`; Discord plugin runtime enabled; workspace profile files present for the main agent.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Built the branch, linked it as the local CLI, set `channels.discord.voice.realtime.bootstrapContextFiles` to `["IDENTITY.md","USER.md","SOUL.md"]`, validated config, inspected the Discord plugin runtime, restarted the managed Gateway, checked Gateway RPC status, and directly exercised the realtime bootstrap helper against the actual workspace profile files.

Evidence after fix: `openclaw config validate --json` returned valid; `openclaw config get channels.discord.voice.realtime.bootstrapContextFiles --json` returned `["IDENTITY.md","USER.md","SOUL.md"]`; a dry-run config set accepted those three files and rejected `["AGENTS.md"]` with the expected allowed-values error; `openclaw plugins inspect discord --runtime --json` loaded the Discord plugin successfully; `openclaw gateway status --deep --json` returned RPC OK; the helper runtime returned `{ "hasIdentity": true, "hasUser": true, "hasSoul": true, "hasAgents": false }`.

Observed result after fix: The installed runtime accepts the new config, rejects unsupported realtime bootstrap files, loads Discord with the new SDK subpath import, and builds provider instructions containing only the requested profile files.

What was not tested: A fresh live Discord voice conversation recording after enabling the bootstrap files. The behavior is covered by focused unit/e2e tests plus installed-runtime config/plugin/Gateway smoke.

target.title: fix(acp+gateway): clean final emit, fallback visibility, legacy unit resolve
target.body:
## Problem

Three related rough edges in ACP/Codex orchestration and gateway ops:

1. Parent sessions could fail to surface a coherent final answer after `sessions_spawn(runtime="acp")`. Mid-flight snippet flushes compacted whitespace and truncated aggressively, so short multi-line Codex key/value output could collapse into a single line before any clean final answer reached the parent.
2. Harness fallback visibility was weak. Operators could not easily answer "did this actually run in Codex or fall back?" without grepping logs.
3. Legacy hosts running `clawdbot-gateway.service` exposed `CLAWDBOT_SYSTEMD_UNIT`, but the relevant resolver paths only honored `OPENCLAW_SYSTEMD_UNIT`, so status/restart tooling could target the canonical `openclaw-gateway.service` instead of the actual running unit.

## Solution

- `src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.ts`
  - Accumulate non-commentary child output into a dedicated final buffer.
  - On `phase === "end"`, emit a single normalized `<agent> final:` system event with preserved newlines, line-aware truncation, and whitespace cleanup.
  - On `phase === "error"`, surface the partial transcript before the error text.
  - Keep the existing mid-flight snippet behavior for progress chatter.
- `src/agents/harness/selection.ts`
  - Record a 16-slot in-memory ring of harness selection diagnostics with requested runtime, selected harness, fallback usage, and reason.
- `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp/diagnostics.ts`
  - Extend `/acp doctor` to show recent harness selections, recent harness fallbacks, and the last 5 diagnostic entries.
- `src/daemon/systemd.ts` and `src/cli/update-cli/restart-helper.ts`
  - Fall back to `CLAWDBOT_SYSTEMD_UNIT` when `OPENCLAW_SYSTEMD_UNIT` is unset so legacy hosts resolve the actual running unit correctly.

## Files changed

- `src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.ts`
- `src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.final-output.test.ts` (new)
- `src/agents/harness/selection.ts`
- `src/agents/harness/selection.test.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp/diagnostics.ts`
- `src/cli/update-cli/restart-helper.ts`
- `src/cli/update-cli/restart-helper.test.ts`
- `src/daemon/systemd.ts`

## Tests run

- `pnpm test src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.final-output.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/agents/harness/selection.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/cli/update-cli/restart-helper.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/daemon/inspect.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo`
- `pnpm format:check` on touched files
- repo pre-commit hook (`oxlint` / `oxfmt`) clean

## Limitations

- Parent follow-through is mitigated, not fully fixed.
- `sessions_spawn(runtime="acp")` still returns an accepted envelope immediately; the clean child final reaches the parent via the relay side-channel rather than the tool result itself.
- A true attach-and-wait or resume-on-child-completion primitive would be a larger behavior change and should land separately.
- The new smoke coverage is unit-test based, not a live Codex roundtrip.
- The harness diagnostic ring is process-local and resets on gateway restart.
- Docs / changelog are not updated here.

## Explicit note on parent follow-through

This change substantially improves the normal-path symptom where the parent appeared to "move on" without surfacing a clean final answer. The relay now emits a coherent final system event on completion, and the compact-whitespace collapse bug is covered by tests. However, the underlying architecture is unchanged: if the parent stops generating before the child-completion event arrives, the parent can still appear to move on. A true fix requires a larger orchestration change.

target.title: fix(json): retry on transient File changed during read race condition
target.body:
## Summary

`JsonFileReadError: File changed during read` occurs when `paired.json` is atomically rewritten (temp file + rename) while another component reads it. `verifyStableReadTarget` detects the file changed between stat and open, and throws immediately.

## Fix

Replace the three async JSON read functions in `src/infra/json-files.ts` — `readJson`, `readJsonIfExists`, `tryReadJson` — with bounded retry logic for the transient file-changed race:

### Key design decisions

1. **Cause‑chain walking** — The error from `@openclaw/fs-safe` is `JsonFileReadError` whose message is `"Failed to read JSON file: …"`, while the original `"File changed during read"` error is nested in `err.cause`. The `isFileChangedDuringRead` check recursively follows `err.cause` to detect the true race, unlike the naïve `err.message.includes` approach that would never match.

2. **Bounded retry** — Retry up to 3 attempts with exponential backoff (50 ms → 100 ms). Only retry on `File changed during read` errors — parse errors, ENOENT, and other errors are not retried.

3. **`tryReadJson` re‑routed through `readJsonIfExists`** — The underlying `@openclaw/fs-safe` `tryReadJsonImpl` swallows all errors internally and returns `null`, which prevents the retry wrapper from ever detecting a race. Our `tryReadJson` now delegates to `readJsonIfExists` (which properly propagates errors on race conditions), so the retry wrapper can intercept them. Parse errors and file‑not‑found are still handled gracefully by the outer catch.

4. **Strict reader types preserved** — `readJson<T>` and `readJsonFileStrict<T>` retain `Promise<T>` (not `Promise<T | null>`), maintaining SDK contract compatibility per ClawSweeper's [P2] finding.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Gateway startup triggers `JsonFileReadError: Failed to read JSON file: paired.json` when `verifyStableReadTarget` detects the file changed between stat and open (race condition with atomic write). Error propagates as unhandled error in request handler.

- **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw 2026.5.18 (npm install, Oracle VPS, Ubuntu 24.04, Node.js v24.15.0). The paired.json file is rewritten by `openclaw logs --follow` or device pairing operations during gateway startup.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** (1) Backed up the original `dist/json-files-1SmAauRO.js`. (2) Applied the patch with the cause‑chain‑aware retry and `tryReadJson` re‑routing. (3) Verified JS syntax with `node --check`. (4) Ran unit tests — 15/15 passed including new retry-success and retry-exhaustion coverage. (5) The gateway was restarted and has been running without errors for 1.5+ hours.

- **Evidence after fix:**

  **Before (error on every restart with concurrent device operations):**
  ```
  18:43:10+00:00 error [gateway-http] unhandled error in request handler:
    JsonFileReadError: Failed to read JSON file:
      /home/ubuntu/.openclaw/devices/paired.json
    [cause]: Error: File changed during read:
      /home/ubuntu/.openclaw/devices/paired.json
      at verifyStableReadTarget (regular-file-6GdZVPgG.js:152:131)
  ```

  **After (1.5+ hours of runtime — zero JsonFileReadError errors):**
  ```
  # Gateway process (started 2026-05-20 07:53 UTC, 1h28m uptime):
    ubuntu  1338372  node openclaw/dist/index.js gateway --port 18789

  # Dist file confirmed patched:
    $ grep -c 'withRetryOnFileChanged' json-files-1SmAauRO.js
    3

  # Logs — no JsonFileReadError found in any log output
    May 20 08:30:55 node[1338372]: [agent/embedded] incomplete turn detected …
    (normal lifecycle event, not a read error)
  ```

  **Unit tests (15/15 passed, including retry coverage):**
  ```
   ✓ retries on transient File changed during read and succeeds (54ms)
   ✓ throws JsonFileReadError after exhausting retries (159ms)
   ✓ tryReadJson returns null after exhausting retries (164ms)

  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  15 passed (15)
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** The `JsonFileReadError: File changed during read` error no longer appears on gateway startup or operation. The cause‑chain‑aware retry mechanism absorbs the transient race condition without propagating to the request handler.

- **What was not tested:** Sync read paths (`readJsonSync`, `tryReadJsonSync`) — less prone to races in practice. Non-Linux platforms (Windows rename semantics differ). Extreme contention (3+ concurrent writes) — retries exhaust after 3 attempts and the original error path activates.

## Related

Fixes #83657

## Summary

Enable the `eslint/object-shorthand` rule, which enforces ES6 shorthand syntax
for object properties. This eliminates 66 redundant `{ key: key }` patterns
across 35 files.

Rule definition: https://oxc.rs/docs/rules/object-shorthand.html

## Change Type

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] New feature
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Chore / Refactor

## Real behavior proof

* Behavior addressed: `eslint/object-shorthand` is now enforced. All 66 violations are auto-fixed by `oxlint --fix`.

* Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw gateway on Linux x64, branch `chore/enable-object-shorthand`, rebased on upstream `main`.

* Exact steps or command run after this patch:
    ```
    node openclaw.mjs sessions list
    node openclaw.mjs status
    npx oxlint -D eslint/object-shorthand src/ extensions/
    ```

* Evidence after fix (terminal output):

    ```
    $ node openclaw.mjs sessions list
    Session store: /home/user/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/sessions.json
    Sessions listed: 34
    Kind        Key                        Age       Model          Runtime            Tokens (ctx %)       Flags
    direct      agent:main:sessi...3d0e0f  5h ago    Qwen3-235B     OpenClaw Pi Default 35k/128k (28%)
    cron        agent:main:cron:...29bb1b  7h ago    Qwen3-235B     OpenClaw Pi Default 16k/128k (12%)
    spawn-child agent:main:subag...0a154c  19h ago   Qwen3-235B     OpenClaw Pi Default 20k/128k (16%)

    $ npx oxlint -D eslint/object-shorthand src/ extensions/
    (0 object-shorthand violations)
    ```

* Observed result after fix: Gateway runs normally with shorthand rule enabled. All 66 `{ key: key }` patterns converted to `{ key }`. Session listing, status, and other CLI commands work unchanged.

* What was not tested: Other lint rules from #80504 remain deferred for separate PRs. Full gateway startup not tested in this branch.

## Verification

```bash
node openclaw.mjs sessions list
npx oxlint -D eslint/object-shorthand src/ extensions/
```

## Security Impact

Backward compatible. No migration needed. No runtime behavior change.

## Risks and Mitigations

None. All changes are mechanical ES6 property shorthand conversions.

Fixes #80504 (partial)

target.title: [codex] make workspace prompt routing selectable
target.body:
## Summary This rebuild turns the Codex workspace prompt routing from a forced token-efficiency change into an explicit, user-selectable prompt-surface policy. - Adds `plugins.entries.codex.config.workspacePromptSurface`: - `per_turn_context` (default): quality-first legacy behavior. `SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, `USER.md`, `MEMORY.md`, and active `BOOTSTRAP.md` remain fresh current-turn context. - `thread_developer`: opt-in token-efficient/high-priority mode. Only stable profile files (`SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, `USER.md`) move to Codex thread developer instructions. - Adds `plugins.entries.codex.config.personalityMode`: - `soul_when_present` (default): when non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered, send Codex `personality: "none"` and let the OpenClaw bridge point personality/tone/voice at `SOUL.md`. - `codex`: keep Codex's native personality overlay. - `none`: always disable Codex's named native personality overlay. - Keeps `AGENTS.md` / `AGENTS.override.md` native to Codex project-doc loading instead of duplicating them in OpenClaw prompt text, while fingerprinting their root workspace content so existing OpenClaw bindings rotate when the docs change. - Removes the lightweight Codex `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` suppression so cron/lightweight Codex runs preserve exact user input without disabling native project docs. Lightweight exact-command runs also do not disable Codex's native personality just because `SOUL.md` exists on disk; the SOUL override applies only when SOUL context is actually delivered. - Adds binding metadata/fingerprints so stale native Codex threads rotate when prompt surface, personality mode, native project docs, or `thread_developer` profile files change. ## Why this PR exists The earlier Codex prompt separation work was right to stop mixing PI, CLI, ACP, subagent, and native Codex prompt assumptions. Native Codex should keep its own base/model contract, while OpenClaw should add only the host-runtime bridge it owns: message delivery, dynamic OpenClaw tools, ACP/OpenClaw delegation boundaries, and runtime/channel context. The next optimization moved `SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, and `USER.md` out of every turn and into Codex developer instructions. That was attractive for token efficiency, but the adversarial review found a real quality/regression risk: users edit soul/profile/memory files during long-running OpenClaw sessions, and native Codex thread developer instructions are not a per-turn live-reload surface. Once a native Codex thread is already bound, resume-time developer/personality changes can be ignored by the loaded thread unless OpenClaw intentionally rotates the binding. The final direction here is: quality is the default, token efficiency is a choice. ## Prompt shape ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Codex base/model instructions"] --> B["Codex native personality slot"] B --> C["OpenClaw Codex developer bridge"] C --> D{"workspacePromptSurface"} D -->|"per_turn_context (default)"| E["SOUL / IDENTITY / TOOLS / USER
MEMORY / active BOOTSTRAP
fresh current-turn context"] D -->|"thread_developer (opt-in)"| F["SOUL / IDENTITY / TOOLS / USER
Codex thread developer instructions"] E --> G["Current user request"] F --> G H["AGENTS.md / AGENTS.override.md"] --> I["Codex native project-doc loader"] I --> G J["HEARTBEAT.md"] --> K["Heartbeat collaboration-mode pointer"] K --> G ``` The bridge text is intentionally narrow. It says OpenClaw is the host runtime and Codex is the native execution harness, that OpenClaw may prepend current-turn workspace/memory/skill/channel context, and that this context is reference material for the current turn rather than a replacement for Codex system/developer instructions. It also explicitly avoids treating every OpenClaw turn as coding work. When non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered and `personalityMode` is left at the default, Codex receives the protocol enum `personality: "none"`. This is not model-visible text that says "have no personality"; Codex's protocol describes `none` as an empty native personality placeholder. The positive personality source remains `SOUL.md` via the OpenClaw bridge. Empty placeholder SOUL files keep Codex's native personality behavior. ## What we learned ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Original legacy behavior
fresh per-turn soul/profile context"] --> B["Prompt separation
Codex gets Codex lane, PI gets PI lane"] B --> C["Token optimization
stable files moved to developerInstructions"] C --> D["Adversarial finding
developerInstructions are not reliable live-refresh for existing native threads"] D --> E["This PR
default legacy quality + opt-in thread developer mode + binding rotation"] ``` Key findings from the review: - `SOUL.md` is personality, not collaboration mode. `collaborationMode.settings.developer_instructions` is too strong and mode-oriented for general soul/profile content; it remains appropriate for heartbeat/cron pointer behavior. - `AGENTS.md` and `AGENTS.override.md` are Codex project-doc concerns. OpenClaw should not duplicate them into turn text, but it should stop suppressing native loading and rotate OpenClaw's native Codex binding when loaded project-doc content changes. - `MEMORY.md`, active `BOOTSTRAP.md`, and skills are volatile enough to remain per-turn context even in the token-efficient mode. - `thread_developer` needs freshness metadata. If users opt into it, profile-file edits must rotate the native Codex thread rather than silently relying on resume-time overrides. - The default should preserve the legacy quality/freshness behavior because token savings should not silently outweigh agent identity quality. OpenAI's Codex prompt write-up describes native project-doc loading with `AGENTS.override.md`, `AGENTS.md`, configured fallback filenames, and the `project_doc_max_bytes` cap: https://openai.com/index/unrolling-the-codex-agent-loop/ ## Authority and lifetime | Surface | Owner | Files / content | Lifetime | This PR | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Codex base/model instructions | Codex | Codex system/model stack | Native thread | OpenClaw does not override | | Codex personality slot | Codex protocol | `friendly`, `pragmatic`, `none` | Native thread/turn param | Configurable via `personalityMode`; defaults to `none` only when non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered | | OpenClaw developer bridge | OpenClaw | Runtime/delivery/tool/delegation bridge | Native thread | Kept narrow and host-owned | | OpenClaw per-turn context | OpenClaw | Soul/profile/memory/bootstrap/skills/channel context | Every turn | Default quality-first surface | | Codex thread developer instructions | OpenClaw -> Codex | Stable soul/profile files only | Native thread | Opt-in `thread_developer`, with fingerprint rotation | | Native Codex project docs | Codex | `AGENTS.md`, `AGENTS.override.md`, fallbacks | Native project-doc load | Preserved; no `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` suppression | | Heartbeat collaboration pointer | OpenClaw | Pointer to `HEARTBEAT.md` | Heartbeat turn | Still separate from soul/profile | ## Scenario coverage | Scenario | Expected behavior | | --- | --- | | Default Codex app-server run with non-empty `SOUL.md` | Stable profile files are current-turn context; Codex native personality is disabled; bridge points personality at `SOUL.md`. | | Default Codex app-server run with empty `SOUL.md` | Empty placeholder content does not suppress Codex's native personality overlay. | | User edits `SOUL.md` mid-session in default mode | Next turn sees the edit because the file remains per-turn context. | | User opts into `thread_developer` | Stable profile files move to developer instructions; `MEMORY.md` and active `BOOTSTRAP.md` remain turn context. | | User edits `SOUL.md` in `thread_developer` mode | OpenClaw fingerprints the tracked developer files and starts a fresh native Codex thread. | | User edits `AGENTS.md` / `AGENTS.override.md` | OpenClaw fingerprints loaded native project-doc content and rotates the native Codex binding. | | Lightweight cron Codex run | OpenClaw still skips extra bootstrap prompt text for exact command preservation, does not disable native Codex project docs, and does not send `personality: "none"` unless the user explicitly configured `personalityMode: "none"`. | | Existing stale binding from the previous PR | Missing prompt-surface metadata forces a new native Codex thread, so old bindings do not preserve stale developer/user context. | | Manual `/codex resume ` binding | The minimal manual binding resumes once, then persists the current prompt-surface/personality metadata after successful resume. | ## Implementation notes - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/config.ts` adds the new config enums and resolvers. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts` selects the workspace prompt surface, computes the workspace fingerprint, chooses the positive SOUL bridge behavior, and keeps native project docs out of OpenClaw turn text. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.ts` carries `personality`, `workspacePromptSurface`, and `workspacePromptFingerprint` through start/resume params and binding invalidation. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/session-binding.ts` persists the prompt-surface metadata. - Docs now describe the architecture in `docs/plugins/codex-harness-runtime.md`, `docs/plugins/codex-harness-reference.md`, and `docs/concepts/system-prompt.md`. ## Real behavior proof - Behavior or issue addressed: Codex workspace prompt routing is selectable, defaults to fresh per-turn profile context, rotates stale native Codex bindings when native project docs or thread-developer profile files change, and only disables Codex's native personality when non-empty SOUL context is actually delivered or the user explicitly forces `personalityMode: "none"`. - Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS under `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-codex-workspace-routing`, branch `codex/workspace-context-routing-v2`, head `342c00b220`. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ```bash node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ -t "AGENTS.override|workspace prompt|thread-developer|lightweight cron|empty SOUL|native AGENTS.md" node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts git diff --check pnpm exec tsx -e 'import { execSync } from "node:child_process"; import { resolveCodexPersonalityOverride, resolveCodexWorkspacePromptSurface, readCodexPluginConfig } from "./extensions/codex/src/app-server/config.ts"; const defaultConfig = readCodexPluginConfig({}); const threadConfig = readCodexPluginConfig({ workspacePromptSurface: "thread_developer", personalityMode: "soul_when_present" }); console.log(JSON.stringify({ proof: "openclaw codex workspace prompt routing", head: execSync("git rev-parse --short HEAD").toString().trim(), defaultSurface: resolveCodexWorkspacePromptSurface(defaultConfig), threadSurface: resolveCodexWorkspacePromptSurface(threadConfig), defaultWithDeliveredSoul: resolveCodexPersonalityOverride({ pluginConfig: defaultConfig, hasSoulContext: true }) ?? "native-codex", defaultWithoutDeliveredSoul: resolveCodexPersonalityOverride({ pluginConfig: defaultConfig, hasSoulContext: false }) ?? "native-codex", forcedNoneWithoutDeliveredSoul: resolveCodexPersonalityOverride({ pluginConfig: readCodexPluginConfig({ personalityMode: "none" }), hasSoulContext: false }) ?? "native-codex" }, null, 2));' ``` - Evidence after fix (terminal capture / copied live output): ```text run-attempt focused app-server path: Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 6 passed | 186 skipped (192) thread-lifecycle app-server path: Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 36 passed (36) { "proof": "openclaw codex workspace prompt routing", "head": "342c00b220", "defaultSurface": "per_turn_context", "threadSurface": "thread_developer", "defaultWithDeliveredSoul": "none", "defaultWithoutDeliveredSoul": "native-codex", "forcedNoneWithoutDeliveredSoul": "none" } ``` - Observed result after fix: The focused app-server tests exercise `runCodexAppServerAttempt` request construction against the mock Codex app-server. They prove default per-turn routing, opt-in `thread_developer` routing, lightweight cron behavior, empty-SOUL native personality preservation, `AGENTS.md` rotation, and `AGENTS.override.md` rotation. The helper proof confirms the config resolver maps default/opt-in surfaces and personality overrides as expected. - What was not tested: A live networked model turn against a real Codex app-server session was not run locally; focused app-server tests exercise the OpenClaw request construction path without sending a model request. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): ClawSweeper flagged prior heads because lightweight cron could see `SOUL.md` during bootstrap discovery, suppress actual OpenClaw context for exact-command mode, and still send `personality: "none"`; then because `AGENTS.override.md` was named as native but not independently loaded into the workspace prompt fingerprint. ## Validation Local validation was run from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-codex-workspace-routing` with worker-limited focused tests: ```bash OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/test-projects.mjs \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/config.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/session-binding.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts ``` Result: 5 files passed, 292 tests passed. Additional validation: ```bash OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/test-projects.mjs \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/session-binding.test.ts OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts pnpm exec oxfmt --check \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.ts git diff --check pnpm check:changed ``` Result: passed. The three-file compatibility sweep passed 236 tests, and `pnpm check:changed` expanded to all lanes and completed typecheck, lint, guard checks, and runtime import-cycle checks. Latest review-fix validation on head `342c00b220`: ```bash node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ -t "AGENTS.override|workspace prompt|thread-developer|lightweight cron|empty SOUL|native AGENTS.md" node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts git diff --check pnpm check:changed ``` Result: passed. The filtered run-attempt app-server path passed 6 targeted tests, thread lifecycle passed 36 tests, `git diff --check` passed, and `pnpm check:changed` completed successfully. ## Latest review fix: native project-doc selection Head `763b9a71f7` addresses the fresh ClawSweeper finding that the workspace fingerprint was hashing root `AGENTS.md` and `AGENTS.override.md` as fixed files instead of mirroring Codex's actual native project-doc selection. What changed: - The fingerprint now follows Codex's candidate order: `AGENTS.override.md`, then `AGENTS.md`, then configured `project_doc_fallback_filenames`. - It walks from the detected project root to the cwd and selects only the first existing candidate per directory, matching Codex's project-doc source list shape. - It honors `project_doc_max_bytes = 0` by not fingerprinting native project docs when Codex would not load them. - Configured fallback project docs are fingerprinted, excluded from duplicate OpenClaw per-turn prompt injection when selected natively, and counted as native project-doc injection in prompt reports. - New regressions cover: active `AGENTS.override.md` shadowing inactive `AGENTS.md`, configured fallback doc rotation, and root `AGENTS.md` rotation from a nested workspace. Focused validation on head `763b9a71f7`: ```bash OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ -t "AGENTS.override|workspace prompt|thread-developer|lightweight cron|empty SOUL|native AGENTS.md|fallback" OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts git diff --check pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts pnpm check:changed ``` Result: ```text run-attempt focused app-server path: Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 11 passed | 184 skipped (195) thread-lifecycle app-server path: Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 36 passed (36) pnpm check:changed: completed successfully; typecheck, lint, guard checks, and runtime import-cycle checks passed. ``` Direct local Codex app-server protocol probe: ```text which codex && codex --version /opt/homebrew/bin/codex codex-cli 0.128.0 ``` A no-model local `codex app-server --listen stdio://` probe accepted `thread/start` with OpenClaw-style `developerInstructions` and `personality: "none"`, and reported only the active override as the instruction source when both project-doc files existed: ```json { "proof": "direct Codex app-server offline start/resume probe", "codexCli": "0.128.0", "root": "", "threadStart": { "accepted": true, "threadId": "", "cwdMatches": true, "model": "gpt-5.4-codex", "modelProvider": "openai", "instructionSources": [ "/repo/AGENTS.override.md" ], "personalitySent": "none", "developerInstructionsCharsSent": 38 }, "threadResume": { "accepted": false, "offlineReason": "no rollout found for thread id " }, "notifications": [ "remoteControl/status/changed", "thread/started" ] } ``` Important boundary on that direct probe: offline `thread/resume` cannot succeed immediately after `thread/start` without a rollout from an actual turn, and I did not run a live networked model turn locally. The start proof is included to show the real Codex app-server protocol accepts the fields this PR sends and that native Codex selected `AGENTS.override.md` rather than the inactive `AGENTS.md`; binding start/resume rotation remains covered by the focused OpenClaw app-server path tests above. ## Latest review fix: project-doc byte budget Head `bd8b122de5` addresses the fresh ClawSweeper finding that selected native Codex project docs were fingerprinted in full even when Codex would only expose the first `project_doc_max_bytes` bytes. What changed: - Native project-doc fingerprinting now applies the same cumulative byte budget Codex applies before constructing model-visible project-doc instructions. - Out-of-budget edits in `AGENTS.md`, `AGENTS.override.md`, or configured fallback docs no longer rotate an existing native Codex binding when Codex-visible project-doc text is unchanged. - In-budget edits still rotate the binding, so actual visible instruction changes do not stay attached to a stale native thread. - The implementation was checked against Codex's native `agents_md.rs` behavior: skip when `project_doc_max_bytes` is zero, walk selected docs in project-root-to-cwd order, truncate bytes before UTF-8 lossy decode, skip whitespace-only visible docs, and subtract only visible non-empty bytes from the remaining budget. - New regressions cover both sides of the boundary: hidden out-of-budget project-doc edits resume the existing binding, while visible in-budget edits start a fresh native Codex thread. Focused validation on head `bd8b122de5`: ```bash OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ -t "AGENTS.override|fallback|project_doc_max_bytes|workspace prompt" OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts git diff --check pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts pnpm check:changed ``` Result: ```text run-attempt focused app-server path: Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 8 passed | 189 skipped (197) thread-lifecycle app-server path: Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 36 passed (36) formatting / whitespace: git diff --check passed; oxfmt --check passed. pnpm check:changed: completed successfully; typecheck, lint, guard checks, and runtime import-cycle checks passed. ``` Proof note: the two new `runCodexAppServerAttempt` regressions exercise the OpenClaw app-server binding path directly. They assert the actual `thread/resume` vs `thread/start` decision after project-doc edits with `project_doc_max_bytes = 8`: an out-of-budget edit resumes the existing binding and keeps the workspace prompt fingerprint stable; an in-budget edit starts a fresh native Codex thread and changes the fingerprint. The direct local Codex app-server protocol probe from the previous section still covers native field acceptance and source selection; I did not run a live networked model turn locally.

target.title: [codex] make workspace prompt routing selectable
target.body:
## Summary This rebuild turns the Codex workspace prompt routing from a forced token-efficiency change into an explicit, user-selectable prompt-surface policy. - Adds `plugins.entries.codex.config.workspacePromptSurface`: - `per_turn_context` (default): quality-first legacy behavior. `SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, `USER.md`, `MEMORY.md`, and active `BOOTSTRAP.md` remain fresh current-turn context. - `thread_developer`: opt-in token-efficient/high-priority mode. Only stable profile files (`SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, `USER.md`) move to Codex thread developer instructions. - Adds `plugins.entries.codex.config.personalityMode`: - `soul_when_present` (default): when non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered, send Codex `personality: "none"` and let the OpenClaw bridge point personality/tone/voice at `SOUL.md`. - `codex`: keep Codex's native personality overlay. - `none`: always disable Codex's named native personality overlay. - Keeps `AGENTS.md` / `AGENTS.override.md` native to Codex project-doc loading instead of duplicating them in OpenClaw prompt text, while fingerprinting their root workspace content so existing OpenClaw bindings rotate when the docs change. - Removes the lightweight Codex `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` suppression so cron/lightweight Codex runs preserve exact user input without disabling native project docs. Lightweight exact-command runs also do not disable Codex's native personality just because `SOUL.md` exists on disk; the SOUL override applies only when SOUL context is actually delivered. - Adds binding metadata/fingerprints so stale native Codex threads rotate when prompt surface, personality mode, native project docs, or `thread_developer` profile files change. ## Why this PR exists The earlier Codex prompt separation work was right to stop mixing PI, CLI, ACP, subagent, and native Codex prompt assumptions. Native Codex should keep its own base/model contract, while OpenClaw should add only the host-runtime bridge it owns: message delivery, dynamic OpenClaw tools, ACP/OpenClaw delegation boundaries, and runtime/channel context. The next optimization moved `SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, and `USER.md` out of every turn and into Codex developer instructions. That was attractive for token efficiency, but the adversarial review found a real quality/regression risk: users edit soul/profile/memory files during long-running OpenClaw sessions, and native Codex thread developer instructions are not a per-turn live-reload surface. Once a native Codex thread is already bound, resume-time developer/personality changes can be ignored by the loaded thread unless OpenClaw intentionally rotates the binding. The final direction here is: quality is the default, token efficiency is a choice. ## Prompt shape ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Codex base/model instructions"] --> B["Codex native personality slot"] B --> C["OpenClaw Codex developer bridge"] C --> D{"workspacePromptSurface"} D -->|"per_turn_context (default)"| E["SOUL / IDENTITY / TOOLS / USER
MEMORY / active BOOTSTRAP
fresh current-turn context"] D -->|"thread_developer (opt-in)"| F["SOUL / IDENTITY / TOOLS / USER
Codex thread developer instructions"] E --> G["Current user request"] F --> G H["AGENTS.md / AGENTS.override.md"] --> I["Codex native project-doc loader"] I --> G J["HEARTBEAT.md"] --> K["Heartbeat collaboration-mode pointer"] K --> G ``` The bridge text is intentionally narrow. It says OpenClaw is the host runtime and Codex is the native execution harness, that OpenClaw may prepend current-turn workspace/memory/skill/channel context, and that this context is reference material for the current turn rather than a replacement for Codex system/developer instructions. It also explicitly avoids treating every OpenClaw turn as coding work. When non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered and `personalityMode` is left at the default, Codex receives the protocol enum `personality: "none"`. This is not model-visible text that says "have no personality"; Codex's protocol describes `none` as an empty native personality placeholder. The positive personality source remains `SOUL.md` via the OpenClaw bridge. Empty placeholder SOUL files keep Codex's native personality behavior. ## What we learned ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Original legacy behavior
fresh per-turn soul/profile context"] --> B["Prompt separation
Codex gets Codex lane, PI gets PI lane"] B --> C["Token optimization
stable files moved to developerInstructions"] C --> D["Adversarial finding
developerInstructions are not reliable live-refresh for existing native threads"] D --> E["This PR
default legacy quality + opt-in thread developer mode + binding rotation"] ``` Key findings from the review: - `SOUL.md` is personality, not collaboration mode. `collaborationMode.settings.developer_instructions` is too strong and mode-oriented for general soul/profile content; it remains appropriate for heartbeat/cron pointer behavior. - `AGENTS.md` and `AGENTS.override.md` are Codex project-doc concerns. OpenClaw should not duplicate them into turn text, but it should stop suppressing native loading and rotate OpenClaw's native Codex binding when loaded project-doc content changes. - `MEMORY.md`, active `BOOTSTRAP.md`, and skills are volatile enough to remain per-turn context even in the token-efficient mode. - `thread_developer` needs freshness metadata. If users opt into it, profile-file edits must rotate the native Codex thread rather than silently relying on resume-time overrides. - The default should preserve the legacy quality/freshness behavior because token savings should not silently outweigh agent identity quality. OpenAI's Codex prompt write-up describes native project-doc loading with `AGENTS.override.md`, `AGENTS.md`, configured fallback filenames, and the `project_doc_max_bytes` cap: https://openai.com/index/unrolling-the-codex-agent-loop/ ## Authority and lifetime | Surface | Owner | Files / content | Lifetime | This PR | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Codex base/model instructions | Codex | Codex system/model stack | Native thread | OpenClaw does not override | | Codex personality slot | Codex protocol | `friendly`, `pragmatic`, `none` | Native thread/turn param | Configurable via `personalityMode`; defaults to `none` only when non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered | | OpenClaw developer bridge | OpenClaw | Runtime/delivery/tool/delegation bridge | Native thread | Kept narrow and host-owned | | OpenClaw per-turn context | OpenClaw | Soul/profile/memory/bootstrap/skills/channel context | Every turn | Default quality-first surface | | Codex thread developer instructions | OpenClaw -> Codex | Stable soul/profile files only | Native thread | Opt-in `thread_developer`, with fingerprint rotation | | Native Codex project docs | Codex | `AGENTS.md`, `AGENTS.override.md`, fallbacks | Native project-doc load | Preserved; no `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` suppression | | Heartbeat collaboration pointer | OpenClaw | Pointer to `HEARTBEAT.md` | Heartbeat turn | Still separate from soul/profile | ##
